modules.title: Introduction to CapCut and the Editing Interface
modules.content:
Imagine capturing a perfect 30-second clip of your friend skateboarding, a breathtaking sunset, or your toddler’s first steps. The raw footage is great, but it contains 10 seconds of you fumbling with the camera, a shaky pan at the beginning, and a sudden cut-off at the end. To transform that raw footage into a shareable moment, you need a tool to trim the fat, keep the good parts, and polish the final result. 

Enter CapCut.

CapCut is a free, wildly popular video editing application developed by ByteDance (the same company behind TikTok). It has rapidly become the go-to editor for beginners and casual creators because it strips away the overwhelming complexity of professional software while retaining a surprisingly powerful set of tools. Whether you want to make short-form vertical videos for social media or long-form horizontal videos for YouTube, CapCut provides an intuitive workspace to bring your ideas to life.

Before you can start slicing and dicing footage, you need to get the software onto your device and learn the language of the editing interface. This chapter will guide you through downloading CapCut, starting your very first project, and decoding the fundamental terminology of video editing.

## Getting CapCut on Your Device

One of CapCut’s biggest advantages is its cross-platform availability. You can edit on your smartphone while commuting, or sit down at a desktop computer for a more precise, large-screen experience. The core concepts remain the same across all devices, though the layout differs slightly.

### Downloading on Mobile (iOS and Android)

If you are using a smartphone or tablet, CapCut is incredibly easy to acquire. 

1. Open your device’s official app store: the **App Store** for Apple devices (iPhone/iPad) or the **Google Play Store** for Android devices.
2. Tap the search bar and type "CapCut."
3. Look for the app with the signature black icon and a stylized blade/image symbol. The developer should be listed as ByteDance.
4. Tap **Get** (iOS) or **Install** (Android). 
5. Once installed, tap **Open** to launch the app. 

You can use the app immediately without an account, though CapCut will occasionally prompt you to log in via TikTok, Google, or Apple to sync projects across devices.

### Downloading on Desktop (Windows and Mac)

If you prefer a traditional editing setup with a mouse and keyboard, the desktop version of CapCut is available for both Windows and Mac computers.

1. Open your web browser and navigate to the official CapCut website (www.capcut.com).
2. The homepage will typically display a prominent "Download" button. It should automatically detect your operating system (Windows or Mac).
3. Click the button to download the installer file.
4. Once the download is complete, open the installer and follow the on-screen prompts to drag the app to your Applications folder (Mac) or run the setup wizard (Windows).

**A note on system requirements:** While CapCut is relatively lightweight compared to professional editors like Adobe Premiere Pro, it still requires a moderately capable computer to play back video smoothly. If your computer is older than five or six years, you may experience some lag when previewing your edits, though the final exported video will usually turn out fine.

## Starting Your First Project

Once you have CapCut open on your preferred device, the first thing you will see is the **Home Screen**. 

On mobile, this screen displays a large "New Project" button at the top, followed by a grid of your previously saved projects (which will be empty right now). On desktop, you will see a similar layout with a prominent "New project" button.

To begin editing, you need to create a project and bring media into it.

1. Click or tap the **New Project** button.
2. Your device’s file browser or camera roll will open. 
3. Select one or two short video clips or photos from your device. 
4. Tap or click **Add** (mobile) or **Open** (desktop) in the bottom right corner.

You are now looking at the main editing workspace. Before we learn what to do with these clips, we need to understand the language of the workspace itself.

## The Language of Video Editing

Video editing has its own jargon. If you understand a few foundational terms, navigating CapCut—and eventually any other video editing software—becomes much easier. Let’s define the most critical terms you will encounter.

### Resolution

**Resolution** refers to the physical size of your video, measured in pixels. A pixel is a tiny dot of light on your screen; thousands of them together make up an image. Resolution is usually expressed as width x height.

*   **1080p (1920 x 1080):** Also known as Full HD, this is the standard resolution for most modern screens and YouTube videos. 
*   **720p (1280 x 720):** Standard HD. It takes up less storage space but is noticeably less sharp than 1080p.
*   **4K (3840 x 2160):** Ultra HD. This is a massive, highly detailed resolution often used by modern smartphones and professional cameras. Editing 4K requires a powerful device.

When you export your final video, you will choose a resolution. For now, just know that higher resolution means a larger, sharper picture, but also larger file sizes.

### Aspect Ratio

While resolution is about the *size* of the video, **Aspect Ratio** is about the *shape* of the video. It is expressed as a ratio of width to height.

*   **16:9:** The standard horizontal rectangle. Think of a television or a YouTube video.
*   **9:16:** The standard vertical rectangle. Think of TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts.
*   **1:1:** A perfect square. Often used for Instagram feed posts.

CapCut allows you to change your project's aspect ratio at any time, which is incredibly useful if you decide to repurpose a horizontal YouTube video into a vertical TikTok.

### The Timeline

The **Timeline** is the heart of your editing workspace. It is the horizontal area at the bottom of your screen (or across the bottom half on desktop) where you arrange your video clips, audio, and text. 

Think of the timeline as a conveyor belt or a digital tape measure. As you move from left to right, you are moving forward in time through your video. The further right a clip is placed on the timeline, the later it will appear in your final video.

### The Playhead

The **Playhead** is a vertical line that moves across your timeline. It acts as your playback marker, indicating exactly which frame of the video you are currently looking at. 

*   When you press the Play button, the playhead moves from left to right, playing your video in real-time.
*   When you click or tap on a specific spot on the timeline, you manually move the playhead to that exact moment. 

Everything to the left of the playhead has already played, and everything to the right is yet to come.

### Tracks

When you import a video clip, it sits on a horizontal lane called a **Track**. A timeline can have multiple tracks stacked on top of one another. 

Think of tracks like transparent sheets laid on top of each other on an overhead projector. 
*   Your main video clips usually sit on the **Main Track** (sometimes called the primary video track). 
*   You can add **Overlay Tracks** above the main track. If you put a video on an overlay track, it will play on top of the main video at the exact same time. 
*   Below the video tracks, you will usually find **Audio Tracks** where music, sound effects, and voiceovers live.

Understanding tracks is vital because it allows you to layer elements—like playing background music while a video plays, or showing two video clips at once.

## Touring the CapCut Workspace

Now that you know the terminology, let's look at where everything is located. Whether you are on mobile or desktop, the CapCut workspace is divided into three main areas: the Preview Window, the Timeline, and the Tool Panels.

### The Preview Window

Located at the top of your screen (or top half, on desktop), the **Preview Window** is exactly what it sounds like. It is the video player that shows you what is happening at the current position of the playhead. 

If you make a cut, add a filter, or insert text, the results are immediately reflected in the Preview Window. This is your canvas. You will use it to visually confirm that your edits look good before finalizing them. You can also press the Play button beneath or beside this window to watch your sequence back.

### The Timeline

Directly below the Preview Window sits the **Timeline**. As mentioned earlier, this is where you do the actual building of your video. 

When you selected your initial media files to start this project, CapCut automatically placed them side-by-side on the Main Track of the timeline. You will see them represented as rectangular blocks with thumbnail images inside them. The length of the block corresponds to the duration of the clip. A 10-second clip will look twice as long as a 5-second clip.

On the desktop version, you will notice a ruler-like line above your clips with numbers (usually representing seconds). This helps you see exactly how long your clips are. On mobile, you can zoom in and out of the timeline by pinching two fingers together or apart on the screen, allowing you to get a microscopic view for precise edits or a bird's-eye view of a long video.

### The Tool Panels

The **Tool Panels** are where the magic happens. This is where you find the buttons and menus to actually manipulate the clips on your timeline. 

**On Mobile:** The primary Tool Panel is a horizontal menu located directly beneath the selected clip on your timeline. When you tap a clip, this menu pops up, offering options like **Split**, **Speed**, **Volume**, and **Delete**. Above the timeline, near the Preview Window, you will find secondary menus for adding new elements: **Audio**, **Text**, **Stickers**, and **Effects**.

**On Desktop:** The layout is more traditional. 
*   At the very top of the screen, you have a menu bar with tabs like **Media**, **Audio**, **Text**, **Transitions**, and **Effects**. Clicking these opens specific panels in the top-left corner of your screen, allowing you to add new elements to your timeline.
*   In the top-right corner, directly above the Preview Window, is the **Inspector Panel** (sometimes called the Properties Panel). When you click on a clip in your timeline, this panel changes to show you the specific adjustments you can make to *that exact clip*—such as volume, speed, color correction, or animation settings.

## A Realistic Scenario: Trimming the Fat

To see how these elements work together, imagine you imported a 15-second clip of your friend skateboarding. You watch it in the Preview Window and realize the first 4 seconds are just you walking to get into position, and the last 3 seconds are you lowering the camera. The actual trick only happens in the middle 8 seconds.

Here is how you would use the workspace to fix it, applying the terms we just learned:

1.  **Locate the start:** Drag your **Playhead** across the **Timeline** until the **Preview Window** shows the exact moment you stop walking and the skateboarder is about to push off.
2.  **Make the first cut:** Select the clip, then tap the **Split** tool (represented by a scissors icon on mobile, or a split button on desktop). This slices the clip into two separate pieces at the playhead.
3.  **Delete the bad footage:** Select the first piece (the 4 seconds of walking) and tap **Delete** (or **Remove**). The clip vanishes from the **Main Track**, and the rest of the video snaps to the left to fill the gap.
4.  **Locate the end:** Drag the **Playhead** to the moment the skateboarder lands the trick, right before you lower the camera. 
5.  **Make the second cut:** Use the **Split** tool again to slice the clip.
6.  **Delete the final bad footage:** Select the last piece (the 3 seconds of camera lowering) and delete it.

In less than a minute, you have used the Timeline, Playhead, Preview Window, and Tool Panel to turn a messy 15-second clip into a tight, 8-second action shot. While Chapter 3 will dive deep into the mechanics of cutting, this scenario illustrates how the interface is designed to facilitate quick, visual storytelling.

## Key Takeaways

*   **CapCut is cross-platform:** It is available for free on iOS, Android, Windows, and Mac, making professional-grade editing tools accessible to anyone.
*   **Resolution defines size, Aspect Ratio defines shape:** Resolution (like 1080p) is the pixel count of your video, while Aspect Ratio (like 16:9 or 9:16) dictates the shape of the frame for different platforms.
*   **The Timeline is your digital workbench:** It operates from left to right, representing the passage of time in your video. 
*   **The Playhead is your guide:** This vertical line shows you exactly where you are in the video, allowing you to navigate to specific frames for precise editing.
*   **Tracks allow for layering:** The Main Track holds your primary video, while Overlay and Audio tracks let you stack additional media and sound on top of one another.
*   **The workspace is divided into three areas:** The Preview Window (your visual canvas), the Timeline (where you arrange media), and the Tool Panels (where you access features to alter the media).

modules.title: Importing and Organizing Media
modules.content:
## Gathering Your Building Blocks

Imagine you are a chef preparing to cook a complex meal. Before you ever turn on the stove, you must gather, wash, and chop your ingredients, laying them out in bowls on the counter so they are within arm's reach when the cooking gets fast. Video editing works exactly the same way. Before you make a single cut, you need to gather your raw video clips, images, and audio files, and arrange them neatly in your editing workspace. 

In the previous chapter, we explored the CapCut interface, learning how to create a New Project and navigate the Timeline, Playhead, and Preview Window. Now, it is time to bring your actual media into that workspace. In video editing, the term **media** refers to any digital file you use to build your video—this includes video clips, photographs, graphics, and audio files like music or voice recordings. 

Understanding how to import media efficiently and organize it before you start cutting will save you hours of frustration. When your "ingredients" are scattered, editing feels chaotic. When they are organized, the creative process becomes smooth and enjoyable.

## Sourcing Your Media: Where Do Videos Come From?

Before you can organize your media, you have to bring it into CapCut. The app offers several ways to do this, depending on where your files currently live. When you open a New Project, you are immediately greeted with a prompt to add media. You generally have three primary sources: your device’s local storage, cloud storage, and stock libraries.

### Importing from Your Device Gallery

The most common way to start is by pulling files directly from the device you are using—whether that is a smartphone, tablet, or computer. 

When you tap the **Add** or **Import** button, CapCut opens your device’s native gallery or file browser. From here, you can simply tap on the files you want to use. 
*   **Multi-selecting:** You do not have to import clips one by one. You can tap multiple clips or images at once. A checkmark usually appears on the items you have selected.
*   **Order of selection matters:** When you select multiple clips from your device gallery and bring them into CapCut, they will appear in the app in the exact order you tapped them. If you want Clip A to come before Clip B, make sure you tap Clip A first.

Once you have selected your files, tap the **Add** or **Import** button (usually in the bottom right corner of the screen) to drop them into your project.

### Importing from Cloud Storage

If you are editing on a mobile device, your phone’s storage might be limited, or your files might live on a different device entirely. This is where cloud storage comes in. **Cloud storage** is a service that holds your files on remote servers rather than on your physical device, allowing you to access them via the internet.

CapCut allows you to connect to cloud services (like Google Drive, iCloud, or Dropbox, depending on your device) to import media. 
1. In the import menu, look for the cloud icon or the option to browse files from other locations.
2. Navigate to your connected cloud service.
3. Select the video or image you want to download into your project.

**A note on cloud media:** When you import a file from the cloud, CapCut has to download a copy of it to your device to edit it. If you have a slow internet connection, this may take a moment. Ensure the file is fully downloaded before you start editing, otherwise the app might lag.

### Using CapCut’s Stock Libraries

Sometimes you do not have the perfect b-roll (supplementary footage) to support your main video, or you need background music but do not own the rights to any songs. CapCut provides built-in **stock libraries**, which are collections of royalty-free media provided directly by the app for you to use.

You can access these libraries from the main import screen or from the Tool Panels below the Timeline:
*   **Stock Video:** High-quality, generic video clips (like a drone shot of a city, or a close-up of coffee pouring) that you can drop into your project to cover cuts or illustrate a point.
*   **Stock Images:** Photographs and graphics you can use as backgrounds or visual aids.
*   **Stock Audio:** A library of music tracks and sound effects. 

To use stock media, simply browse the categories, tap on a clip to preview it, and select the download or plus icon to bring it into your project. 

## Understanding Track Hierarchy: Where Does Media Go?

Once you import media, it has to live somewhere in your editing workspace. In Chapter 1, we briefly introduced the concept of Tracks. Now, we need to look at how different types of media interact on these tracks, as this is the foundation of how CapCut organizes your files.

### The Main Video Track

The **Main Track** is the backbone of your video. Think of it as the primary timeline of events. When you import a batch of video clips and drop them onto the timeline, they automatically snap together end-to-end on this Main Track. 

If you have a video of a person talking, that interview footage belongs on the Main Track. It is the base layer. If you delete a clip from the Main Track, the subsequent clips will slide over to fill the gap, keeping the timeline continuous. Every project must have a Main Track; you cannot have a video without it.

### Overlay Tracks

If the Main Track is the foundation of a house, **Overlay Tracks** are the decorations inside the rooms. An overlay is any video or image that plays *on top of* the Main Track. 

Why would you want media on top of other media? 
*   **Picture-in-picture:** Showing a reaction video in a small box in the corner while your main video plays.
*   **B-roll coverage:** Showing a video of a beautiful sunset while a voiceover from the Main Track continues to play underneath.
*   **Graphics:** Placing a logo or a watermark in the corner of your screen.

When you add an overlay, it sits on a track directly above the Main Track. Overlays do not push Main Track clips out of the way. Instead, they float above them. You can have multiple Overlay Tracks stacked on top of each other, allowing for complex compositions where several videos or images are visible at the same time.

### Audio Tracks

**Audio Tracks** handle the sound elements of your video. Any video clip you place on the Main Track automatically brings its original sound with it on the same track. However, when you import standalone audio files—like a downloaded MP3 song or a voice memo you recorded—they are placed on dedicated Audio Tracks, which sit below the Main Video Track. 

Keeping your audio on separate tracks allows you to adjust the volume of your background music independently from your main video’s dialogue.

## Organizing in the Media Panel

When you import media into CapCut, it does not immediately disappear into the timeline forever. It is first stored in the **Media Panel** (sometimes called the Project Panel or Import Panel), which is the library of all the assets you have brought into this specific project. 

On a computer, this panel is usually located in the top left corner of the screen. On a mobile device, it appears as a horizontal scrolling list of thumbnails just above the Timeline.

### Arranging Clips Before Editing

One of the biggest mistakes beginners make is immediately dragging every single clip onto the Timeline and then trying to make sense of the mess. A much more efficient workflow is to arrange and review your clips in the Media Panel *before* bringing them to the Timeline.

Here is a practical scenario: Imagine you just returned from a weekend trip and shot 45 different video clips on your phone. You want to make a one-minute travel montage.

1.  **Import everything:** Bring all 45 clips into the Media Panel, but do not drag them to the Timeline yet.
2.  **Review and trim the fat:** Tap through the clips in the Media Panel. If a clip is entirely blurry or unusable, delete it from the panel right now. 
3.  **Rename for clarity (Desktop):** If you are on a computer, you can often right-click a clip in the Media Panel and rename it. Naming a clip "Sunset_Beach" makes it much easier to find than "IMG_4829.MP4".
4.  **Group by type:** Mentally (or physically, by rearranging the thumbnails) group your clips. Put all your wide landscape shots together, and your close-up food shots together. 

By organizing your media panel first, you create a logical roadmap for your video. When you finally start dragging clips to the Timeline in the next chapter, you will know exactly where everything is.

## The Art of Replacement: Swapping Clips Safely

As you edit, you will often change your mind. You might place a video clip on the Timeline, spend time adjusting its speed, applying a color filter, and perfectly positioning it—only to realize you actually prefer a different, but similar, clip you shot of the same subject.

If you delete the clip and start over, you lose all the edits you just made. Fortunately, CapCut has a powerful feature that solves this: **Replace**.

The Replace function allows you to swap out a clip on the Timeline with a different clip from your Media Panel, while keeping all the edits, effects, and adjustments you applied to the original clip intact.

### How to Use the Replace Function

The exact steps vary slightly depending on whether you are using CapCut on a mobile device or a desktop, but the core concept remains the same.

**On Mobile:**
1. Tap the clip on the Timeline that you want to swap out.
2. In the Tool Panel (the menu of icons below the Timeline), scroll until you find the **Replace** icon (it usually looks like two overlapping squares or a recycling symbol).
3. Tap **Replace**. CapCut will ask if you want to replace the clip from your device gallery or from the stock library.
4. Select your source, then tap the new clip you want to use.
5. CapCut will give you two options: **Replace** or **Overwrite**. 
    *   If you choose standard Replace, the new clip will take on the exact duration and position of the old clip. If the new clip is longer, it will be trimmed to match. If it is shorter, CapCut will slow it down or freeze a frame to match the length (depending on settings).
    *   You can usually drag the new clip left or right within that slot to choose a different starting point before confirming.

**On Desktop:**
1. Right-click the clip on the Timeline you wish to change.
2. Select **Replace** from the drop-down menu.
3. Choose **Replace clip** to swap it with a clip from your Media Panel, or **Replace with placeholder** if you want to leave a blank slot to fill later.
4. Click the new clip from your Media Panel to execute the swap.

### Why Replacement is a Lifesaver

Let’s look at another scenario. You are editing a cooking video. On the Main Track, you have a clip of chopped onions falling into a sizzling pan. You have perfectly timed this clip to be exactly 3 seconds long, and you have added a warm color filter to make the food look appetizing. 

Later, you realize the lighting in that shot was actually a bit too dark, but you have a second take of the exact same action that is brighter. Instead of deleting the dark clip, losing your 3-second timing and your color filter, and starting over with the bright clip, you simply use the **Replace** tool. You select the bright clip from your gallery, hit Replace, and CapCut swaps the video while keeping the 3-second duration and the warm color filter perfectly intact. 

This tool is essentially an "undo" button for your initial importing choices, allowing you to experiment freely without fear of having to redo tedious editing work.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Media** is the umbrella term for all video, image, and audio files used in a project.
*   You can import media from three main sources: your device’s local gallery, cloud storage (which requires an internet connection to download), and CapCut’s built-in stock libraries.
*   The **Main Track** is the foundational layer of your video where primary clips sit end-to-end.
*   **Overlay Tracks** sit above the Main Track and allow videos or images to play simultaneously on screen, useful for picture-in-picture and b-roll.
*   **Audio Tracks** sit below the video tracks and hold your music and sound effects independently of the video.
*   The **Media Panel** acts as your digital ingredient bowl. Organizing, reviewing, and deleting unusable clips in the Media Panel *before* moving them to the Timeline makes the editing process much smoother.
*   The **Replace** tool allows you to swap a clip on the Timeline with a new one from your media library without losing the timing, effects, and adjustments you already applied to the original clip.

modules.title: Core Cutting and Sequencing
modules.content:
## The Art of the Cut

Imagine you are watching a suspenseful movie. The hero slowly reaches for the door handle. The camera cuts to a shadow moving in the dark. It cuts back to the hero’s widening eyes. 

That rapid change from one image to another is called a **cut**. It is the most fundamental transition in all of video editing. Long before we had digital effects, fancy animations, or complex sound design, filmmakers were telling compelling stories simply by cutting pieces of film and taping them back together. 

In CapCut, your raw footage is like a block of marble. You have the rough, unshaped clips you imported in the previous module. Now, it is time to take out the chisel. Core cutting and sequencing is the process of carving away the mistakes, keeping the best moments, and arranging them in a specific order to build your video’s narrative flow. 

Before we make our first cut, we need to understand how to navigate the workspace efficiently.

## Navigating the Timeline Workspace

In Chapter 1, we introduced the **Timeline**—the horizontal workspace at the bottom of your screen where your video is assembled. We also introduced the **Playhead**, the vertical white line that indicates your current position in the video. 

To cut accurately, you must be able to move the playhead to exact frames. Here are the primary ways to navigate your timeline in CapCut:

*   **Tapping the Timeline:** You can tap anywhere on a clip in the timeline, and the playhead will instantly snap to that location.
*   **Dragging the Playhead:** Place your finger directly on the playhead's handle (usually a small circle or dot at the top of the line) and drag it left or right. 
*   **The Play Button:** Tap the Play button beneath the **Preview Window** to play your video from the playhead's current position. As it plays, the playhead will move across the timeline in real-time.
*   **Frame-by-Frame Navigation:** For precise editing, CapCut provides small arrow buttons (often looking like `◀◀` and `▶▶`) near the play button. Tapping these moves the playhead exactly one frame at a time. This is essential when you are trying to cut at the exact moment an action begins or ends.

## Trimming: Removing the Unwanted Edges

When you film a video, it rarely starts exactly when the action begins. You might have three seconds of you walking up to the camera, adjusting your hair, and pressing record before you actually say, "Hello!" Similarly, the end of the clip might feature you fumbling for the stop button. 

**Trimming** is the act of shortening a clip by removing its beginning or end. In CapCut, this is done by dragging the edges of the clip.

### How to Trim a Clip

1.  **Select the Clip:** Tap once on the clip you want to trim in your Main Track. It will be highlighted with a white border.
2.  **Locate the Handles:** Look at the left and right edges of the highlighted clip. You will see thick, white vertical bars. These are your **trim handles**.
3.  **Drag the Handle:** Press and hold the left trim handle, then drag it to the right to remove the beginning of the clip. As you drag, the Preview Window will show you the exact frame you are cutting on. Let go when the unwanted footage is gone.
4.  **Repeat for the End:** Grab the right trim handle and drag it to the left to remove the ending of the clip.

### The Scenario: The Vlog Intro

Let’s look at a concrete example. You imported a 15-second clip of you introducing your video. 
*   Seconds 0 to 4: You are setting the camera on the tripod. (Unwanted)
*   Seconds 4 to 13: You say, "Hey everyone, welcome back to my channel!" (Wanted)
*   Seconds 13 to 15: You trip over the tripod cord. (Unwanted)

To fix this, you select the clip, grab the left trim handle, and drag it right until the Preview Window shows you standing still, right at the four-second mark. Then, you grab the right trim handle and drag it left until the Preview Window shows you finishing your sentence, right before you trip. You have just trimmed a 15-second clip down to a clean 9-second intro.

## The Split Tool: Dividing a Single Clip

Trimming is great for cleaning up the start and end of a clip, but what if there is a mistake in the middle? Imagine you are filming a tutorial, and right in the middle of explaining a concept, your phone rings. You don't want to delete the first half of your explanation, nor do you want to keep the ringing phone in the video.

This is where the **Split** tool comes in. Splitting allows you to divide a single, continuous clip into two or more separate segments. 

### How to Use the Split Tool

1.  **Position the Playhead:** Move the playhead to the exact frame where you want to make the division. In the phone-ringing scenario, you would move the playhead to the exact moment the phone starts ringing.
2.  **Select the Clip:** Tap the clip in the timeline so it is highlighted.
3.  **Tap Split:** Look at the toolbar at the bottom of the screen (below the timeline). Tap the **Split** icon (it usually looks like a small rectangle with a dashed vertical line through it). 

Instantly, your one clip becomes two clips sitting side-by-side on the timeline. The split happens precisely at the playhead. You can now select the second clip, move the playhead to where you start talking again, and split it a second time. This isolates the ringing phone into its own separate clip.

*Note: If you are using CapCut on a desktop computer, you can often achieve this same action by selecting the clip and pressing the `S` key on your keyboard, or `Cmd+B` on a Mac.*

## Managing Your Segments: Delete, Duplicate, and Rearrange

Once you start splitting your clips, your timeline will fill up with multiple segments. Now you are playing with building blocks. CapCut gives you the tools to manage these blocks however you see fit.

### Deleting Clips

If you split your footage to isolate a mistake, the next step is to remove that mistake entirely. 

1.  Tap the unwanted segment in the timeline to select it.
2.  Tap the **Delete** icon in the bottom toolbar (it looks like a trash can).
3.  The clip disappears, and any clips to the right of it will automatically slide left to fill the gap.

*Beginner Tip:* If you accidentally delete the wrong clip, don't panic! CapCut has an **Undo** button (a curved arrow pointing left) at the top of the screen. Tap it to reverse your last action.

### Duplicating Clips

Sometimes, you want a piece of footage to repeat. For instance, you might have a beautiful five-second clip of a sunset that you want to loop to make it last ten seconds. Instead of going back to your media library and re-importing the clip, you can **Duplicate** it.

1.  Select the clip you want to repeat.
2.  Tap the **Duplicate** button in the bottom toolbar (it usually looks like two overlapping squares).
3.  CapCut instantly creates a copy of the clip and places it immediately to the right of the original on the timeline. 

### Rearranging Clips

The true power of digital editing lies in non-linear editing—the ability to place clips in any order you wish, regardless of when they were filmed. 

To rearrange a segment:
1.  Press and hold your finger on the clip you want to move. 
2.  Drag the clip to the left or right along the timeline. As you drag, you will see the other clips "part" to make room for the one you are holding.
3.  Release your finger to drop the clip into its new position.

### The Scenario: The Cooking Video

Let’s apply all three of these tools. You are editing a simple cooking video. You have three clips: chopping vegetables, boiling water, and tasting the final dish. 

1.  **Split and Delete:** You play the "chopping vegetables" clip and realize you paused for ten seconds in the middle to wipe your brow. You move the playhead to the start of the pause, hit **Split**, move the playhead to the end of the pause, hit **Split** again, select the isolated pause, and **Delete** it. The chopping action now looks continuous.
2.  **Rearrange:** You realize you want the video to start with a shot of the final dish to tease the audience. You press and hold the "tasting the dish" clip, drag it all the way to the left of the timeline, and drop it before the chopping clip. 
3.  **Duplicate:** The shot of the final dish is only three seconds long, but you want it on screen a bit longer to let the audience appreciate it. You select the clip and hit **Duplicate**. You now have six seconds of the dish before the video cuts to the chopping.

## The Snap Feature: Aligning Without Gaps

As you start deleting, splitting, and rearranging clips, you might notice a frustrating issue: tiny, almost invisible gaps between your clips. If there is a gap between two video clips, your final video will show a black screen for that fraction of a second, which looks highly unprofessional.

To prevent this, CapCut utilizes a feature called **Snap** (sometimes referred to as magnetic snapping). 

**Snap** is a behavior built into the timeline. When Snap is active, clips act like they have magnets inside them. As you drag a clip near another clip, the clip you are dragging will suddenly "jump" or snap perfectly into place against the other clip, ensuring there is zero empty space between them.

### How to Use Snap

In most versions of CapCut, Snap is turned on by default. When you drag a clip and get close to another clip or the playhead, you will feel a slight resistance, and the clip will lock into place. 

If you ever want to turn Snap off—perhaps you are trying to place a clip very precisely and the magnetic snapping keeps pulling it too far—look at the top right of the timeline toolbar. You will usually find a small icon that looks like a magnet or says **Snap**. Tapping this toggles the feature on and off. 

For beginners, it is highly recommended to keep Snap turned on. It is the easiest way to ensure your clips are perfectly aligned and your video flows smoothly from one shot to the next without black flashes.

## Building Narrative Flow

Editing is not just about removing mistakes; it is about controlling the viewer's experience. The order of your clips and the exact moments you choose to cut dictate the **narrative flow**—the rhythm and pacing of your story.

Think of cutting like writing a sentence. Every clip is a word. If you put the words in the wrong order, the sentence doesn't make sense. If you leave in too many extra words, the sentence drags. 

When building your sequence, ask yourself:
*   **Does this clip advance the story?** If a clip doesn't provide new information, emotional context, or visual interest, consider deleting it.
*   **When should the cut happen?** A common technique is to cut on action. If someone is opening a door, you might show them reaching for the handle in one clip, and cut to the door swinging open from the other side in the next clip. The motion distracts the eye from the cut, making it feel seamless.
*   **Is the sequence logical?** If you show a person reacting to something in Clip A, the viewer will expect to see what they are reacting to in Clip B. 

By using the Trim tool to clean up your clips, the Split tool to isolate the best moments, and the rearrange feature to put them in a logical order, you are no longer just showing raw footage. You are directing the viewer's attention and telling a story.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Navigation is foundational:** Use the Playhead, the Play button, and the frame-by-frame arrows to navigate your timeline accurately before making cuts.
*   **Trimming cleans the edges:** Use the white trim handles on the left and right sides of a selected clip to remove unwanted footage from the beginning or end.
*   **Splitting divides clips:** Use the Split tool to cut a single clip into multiple segments, allowing you to isolate mistakes or isolate specific actions in the middle of a clip.
*   **Timeline management:** You can Delete unwanted segments, Duplicate clips you want to repeat, and press-and-drag to Rearrange clips into your desired order.
*   **Snap prevents gaps:** The Snap feature acts like a magnet, automatically aligning clips next to each other to ensure there are no black flashes in your final video.
*   **Narrative flow:** Cutting and sequencing are about more than just cleaning up footage; they are the primary tools you use to control the pacing and logic of your video's story.

modules.title: Audio Integration and Sound Design
modules.content:
## The Invisible Half of Your Video

Imagine watching your favorite movie scene with the volume completely muted. The actors are moving, things are exploding, but there is no music, no footsteps, no rustling leaves. It feels hollow, doesn't? That is because audio carries at least half of the emotional weight of any video. A suspenseful scene isn't suspenseful because of the dark lighting alone; it’s suspenseful because of the low, rumbling drone underneath the dialogue. 

Up to this point, you have learned how to import your media, arrange it on the Timeline, and trim the clips to create a visual sequence. However, your project is currently a silent film. It is time to give your video a voice. 

In this chapter, we will explore how to layer audio using CapCut's **Audio Tracks**. You will learn how to pull audio from existing videos, add background music, record a voiceover, and use fades to ensure everything sounds professional and balanced.

## Understanding Audio Tracks in CapCut

In Chapter 2 and 3, we discussed the **Timeline** and how your video clips sit on the **Main Track**. When you add audio to a project, CapCut places it on an **Audio Track**. 

Just like **Overlay Tracks** allow you to stack multiple videos on top of each other, Audio Tracks allow you to stack multiple sounds. A standard video might have three audio tracks playing simultaneously:
1.  The original sound recorded with the video clip (dialogue or ambient room noise).
2.  A background music track.
3.  A voiceover narration.

Understanding that audio lives on these separate, stackable layers is the foundation of sound design. Because they are separate, you can edit, move, and adjust the volume of one sound without affecting the others.

## Extracting Audio from Existing Video Clips

Sometimes, the perfect sound effect is hiding inside a video you already recorded. For example, imagine you recorded a video of your dog barking, but the camera was shaky. You want to use the bark sound over a smooth, pre-recorded video of your dog sitting nicely. 

To do this, you need to separate the audio from the video file. This process is called **extracting audio**.

When you extract audio, CapCut makes a copy of the sound, deletes the picture, and places the audio on a new Audio Track directly beneath the original video clip. 

### How to Extract Audio
1. Tap on the video clip in your Main Track to select it. A toolbar will appear at the bottom of the screen.
2. Scroll through the bottom toolbar until you find the **Extract audio** button (it usually looks like a small waveform icon).
3. Tap it. 
4. The video clip will turn into a green audio clip on a new Audio Track, and the visual portion of that clip will disappear from the Main Track.

*Note: If you want to keep the original video clip but also have a separate copy of its audio, simply duplicate the video clip first, extract the audio from the duplicate, and then delete the visual part of the duplicate.*

## Adding Background Music

Background music sets the tone for your video. It tells the viewer exactly how they should be feeling. CapCut gives you two ways to add music: using their built-in library or importing your own external files.

### Using CapCut’s Audio Library
CapCut has a massive, built-in library of royalty-free music and sound effects. **Royalty-free** means you have the legal right to use the audio in your videos without paying licensing fees to the artist. This is incredibly important if you plan to post your videos to platforms like YouTube or TikTok, which actively scan for copyrighted music.

1. Navigate to the main toolbar at the bottom of the screen and tap **Audio**. 
2. Tap **Sounds**. This opens the CapCut audio library.
3. Here, you can browse by category (e.g., Pop, Vlog, Cinematic) or use the search bar to find a specific mood or genre.
4. When you find a track you like, tap the **Play** icon next to it to preview it.
5. Tap the **Download** icon (a downward arrow) next to the song to save it to your device.
6. Once downloaded, tap the **+** icon next to the track to add it to your Timeline.

The music will appear as an orange or green audio clip on a new Audio Track beneath your video.

### Importing External Audio Files
If you have your own music files (like an MP3 you downloaded or a voice memo you recorded on your phone), you can easily bring them into CapCut.

1. Tap **Audio** in the bottom toolbar.
2. Tap **Sounds**.
3. Instead of browsing the library, tap the **Folder** icon or "Your Sounds" tab at the top of the screen.
4. Tap **From device** (or a similarly worded import button).
5. Navigate through your phone’s file browser to select the audio file you want to import.

The external file will now be placed on your Timeline, ready for editing.

## Recording a Voiceover Directly in CapCut

If you are making a tutorial, a documentary, or a vlog, you will likely need to speak over your footage. A **voiceover** is a narration spoken by someone not visible in the video. 

Instead of recording your voice in a separate app and importing it, CapCut allows you to record directly onto your Timeline. This is highly efficient because you can watch your video play and narrate it in real time.

### How to Record a Voiceover
1. Move your **Playhead** to the exact spot on the Timeline where you want your narration to begin.
2. Tap **Audio** in the bottom toolbar.
3. Tap **Record** (usually represented by a microphone icon).
4. A large red record button will appear. When you are ready, tap and hold (or simply tap, depending on your version) the button to begin recording. 
5. Speak clearly into your phone’s microphone. You can press the Play button on the Timeline to watch your video while you record.
6. Tap the stop button when you are finished.

CapCut will automatically place your recording on a new Audio Track. If you need to record another segment later in the video, just move the Playhead and repeat the process.

*Tip: For the best audio quality, record your voiceover in a quiet room with minimal echo. If you have earbuds with a built-in microphone, use them—they usually sound much better than your phone’s internal microphone.*

## Adjusting Volume Levels

Now that you have multiple audio tracks—perhaps a video’s original sound, background music, and a voiceover—you have a new problem: they are all fighting to be heard. If the background music is too loud, the viewer won't be able to hear your voiceover. 

**Balancing audio levels** is the act of adjusting the volume of individual tracks so they blend together harmoniously. As a general rule, human speech should always be the loudest element, with music and ambient noise sitting quietly underneath it.

### How to Adjust Volume
1. Tap on any audio clip (or your video clip, which contains the original audio) on your Timeline to select it.
2. Tap the **Volume** button in the bottom toolbar.
3. A slider will appear. Drag the slider left to decrease the volume (making it quieter) or right to increase it (making it louder). The default is 100.
4. Tap the checkmark to confirm your choice.

If you have background music playing under a voiceover, try dropping the music’s volume down to 15 or 20. This keeps the music present enough to set the mood, but quiet enough that it doesn't overpower the spoken words.

## Applying Fade In and Fade Out Effects

Have you ever noticed how jarring it is when a loud song suddenly starts blaring the exact moment a video begins? Or when music cuts off abruptly mid-sentence? These sudden audio changes feel unprofessional. 

A **fade** is a gradual increase or decrease in audio volume. A **Fade In** starts the audio at zero volume and slowly raises it to normal volume. A **Fade Out** does the opposite, slowly lowering the volume to zero. Using fades makes your audio transitions smooth and seamless.

### How to Apply Fades
1. Tap on the audio clip you want to adjust on your Timeline.
2. Tap the **Fade** button in the bottom toolbar.
3. You will see two sliders: **Fade in** and **Fade out**.
4. Drag the **Fade in** slider to the right. You will see a number representing seconds (e.g., 1.0s, 2.0s). This means the audio will take that amount of time to reach full volume.
5. Drag the **Fade out** slider to the right to set how long it takes for the audio to drop to silence at the end of the clip.
6. Tap the checkmark to apply.

*Scenario Example:* Imagine you have a 10-second clip of waves crashing on a beach, and you want to put a calm piano track over it. If you just drop the piano track in, it hits the viewer too hard. By adding a 2-second Fade In to the piano track, the music gently swells up beneath the sound of the waves, creating a relaxing, professional transition. At the end of the video, adding a 3-second Fade Out ensures the music doesn't cut off awkwardly when the video ends.

## A Note on Sound Effects

While background music and voiceovers form the backbone of your audio, **sound effects** (SFX) add the finishing touches. A sound effect is a specific, short audio clip used to emphasize an action—like a "whoosh" when text appears on screen, a "pop" when a sticker pops up, or a cash register "cha-ching" when showing a product.

You add sound effects exactly the same way you add background music: by tapping **Audio > Sounds** and searching the CapCut library. Instead of searching for music genres, you can search for words like "whoosh," "pop," "click," or "camera." 

When using sound effects, volume balancing is critical. A sound effect should be noticeable, but it should never make the viewer flinch. If a whoosh is too loud, tap the sound effect clip, go to **Volume**, and turn it down to 40 or 50. 

## Putting It All Together: A Simple Audio Workflow

Let’s walk through a realistic scenario of how you might layer audio for a 30-second travel vlog:

1.  **The Base Layer:** You have your video clips sequenced on the Main Track. The clips have some wind noise in the background, so you tap the video clips, go to **Volume**, and turn the original audio down to 10.
2.  **The Music Layer:** You tap **Audio > Sounds**, search for "upbeat travel," and add a royalty-free song to your project. It’s a bit too loud, so you tap the music track, go to **Volume**, and set it to 30. You also add a 2-second Fade In at the beginning and a 3-second Fade Out at the end of the song.
3.  **The Voiceover Layer:** You move your Playhead to the 5-second mark, tap **Audio > Record**, and narrate a quick introduction: "Welcome to my trip to the mountains!" You stop recording. Because your voice is the most important audio, you leave its volume at 100. To ensure the music doesn't compete with your voice, you might even split the music track right where your voice starts, and lower the volume of that specific section of music to 15.
4.  **The SFX Layer:** You want to emphasize a quick transition where the camera pans to a mountain peak. You search the audio library for "whoosh," add it right at the moment of the pan, and turn the volume down to 50.

By stacking these tracks and balancing their volumes, you have transformed a silent sequence of clips into an immersive, engaging video experience.

## Key Takeaways
*   Audio carries at least half the emotional weight of a video and is layered using **Audio Tracks** beneath the Main Track.
*   **Extracting audio** allows you to separate the sound from a video clip so you can use it independently.
*   CapCut offers a built-in, **royalty-free** library of music and sound effects, or you can import external audio files from your device.
*   You can record a **voiceover** directly in CapCut by moving the Playhead to the desired start point and using the Audio > Record function.
*   **Balancing audio levels** is crucial; human speech should always be the loudest element, with background music and ambient noise turned down so they don't compete.
*   Applying a **Fade In** or **Fade Out** gradually increases or decreases audio volume, preventing abrupt, jarring sound transitions.

modules.title: Text, Titles, and Auto Captions
modules.content:
Imagine scrolling through your social media feed on a crowded train. The volume on your phone is muted, but a video catches your eye. Without hearing a single word, you understand exactly what the creator is saying because the text dynamically pops up on the screen in sync with their voice. 

In video editing, audio and visuals are only two-thirds of the equation. Text bridges the gap between the two, providing context, emphasis, and accessibility. Whether you are adding an attention-grabbing title at the beginning of your video, labeling a specific location, or generating subtitles so viewers can watch without sound, text is a critical component of modern editing.

CapCut provides a robust suite of text tools that allow you to do everything from manually typing out a simple sentence to automatically transcribing your entire video’s audio. In this chapter, we will explore how to add textual elements to your timeline, style them to match your video’s aesthetic, and leverage AI-powered auto-captions to do the heavy lifting for you.

## Adding Text Boxes to the Timeline

Just like your video and audio clips, text exists on your Timeline as its own distinct element. When you add text to a project, CapCut places it on a dedicated **Text Track**. Because it sits on its own track, it can hover above your main video and audio without permanently altering the underlying media.

### Creating Your First Text Box

To add text to your project, you will use the main navigation menu located at the bottom of the CapCut interface. 

1. Move your **Playhead** to the exact spot on the **Timeline** where you want the text to appear.
2. Tap the **Text** icon from the bottom menu.
3. Select **Add text**.
4. A cursor will appear on the screen preview, and a text box will be added to your Timeline. Type your desired text using your device’s keyboard.
5. Tap the checkmark (or **Done**) to confirm the text.

Once added, you will notice a new clip on your Timeline marked with a "T" icon. This is your text clip. Because it sits on a separate track above your **Main Track** video, it behaves much like a standard video clip. You can select it, tap it, and use the same trimming techniques you learned in *Core Cutting and Sequencing* to adjust its start and end points. 

### Modifying Text Duration

By default, CapCut assigns a standard duration (usually a few seconds) to any new text box. However, you rarely want text to appear and disappear arbitrarily. The duration of your text should be dictated by how long the viewer needs to read it, or how long the corresponding audio plays.

There are two primary ways to modify a text clip’s duration:

*   **Manual Trimming:** Tap the text clip on your Timeline to select it. White handles will appear on the left and right edges of the clip. Tap and drag the right handle to extend the text, or drag the left handle to shorten it. This is ideal for quick, visual adjustments.
*   **Precise Duration Control:** If you need a title to stay on screen for exactly three seconds, manual dragging can be imprecise. Tap the text clip, then look for the **Duration** icon in the bottom menu (it looks like a small clock). Tapping this brings up a numeric keypad. You can type in the exact duration in seconds and milliseconds, ensuring your text aligns perfectly with your edit.

**A Note on Readability:** When setting duration, a good rule of thumb is to leave the text on screen long enough for you to read it aloud at a normal, relaxed pace. If you have to rush to read a title before it vanishes, your viewers will certainly struggle to read it too.

## Customizing Text Styles

Adding text is only the first step. Unformatted, default text can look out of place against the dynamic visuals of your video. CapCut allows you to customize the font, color, size, and outline to ensure your text fits the mood and aesthetic of your project.

To access these styling tools, tap your text clip on the Timeline, and then tap the **Edit** (or pencil) icon that appears in the bottom menu. This opens the text styling workspace.

### Fonts and Sizes

*   **Font:** A font is the specific design of the lettering. CapCut offers dozens of built-in fonts ranging from clean, professional sans-serif styles to playful, handwritten scripts. Scroll horizontally through the font menu to preview how your text looks in each style. 
*   **Size:** You can adjust the size of your text by dragging the Size slider. Alternatively, you can use the **Pinch-to-Zoom** gesture directly on the screen preview. Place two fingers on the text box and move them apart to make the text larger, or pinch them together to shrink it.

### Colors and Backgrounds

Color is one of the most powerful tools for making your text readable. 

*   **Color:** Tap the Color icon (usually represented by a colored circle) to open the color palette. You can choose from preset colors or use the eyedropper tool to select a color directly from your video. For example, if your subject is wearing a bright red jacket, you can use the eyedropper to make your text the exact same shade of red, tying the graphic to the visuals.
*   **Background:** Sometimes, your text sits over a busy, complex background (like a moving crowd or a textured wall), making it hard to read. CapCut allows you to add a solid color background behind your text box. You can adjust the opacity (transparency) of this background so it appears as a subtle banner rather than a harsh, solid block.

### Outlines and Shadows

Even with a solid color, text can sometimes blend into the background. This is where outlines and shadows become essential.

*   **Outline:** An outline (sometimes called a stroke) is a border drawn around the edge of your letters. CapCut lets you choose the color and thickness of this outline. A thin, dark outline around white text is a standard industry trick to ensure the text remains legible regardless of what happens in the background video.
*   **Shadow:** Adding a shadow casts a faint duplicate of your text slightly offset behind it. This creates a sense of depth, making the text "pop" off the screen. You can usually adjust the shadow’s color, blur (how soft the edges are), and angle (the direction the shadow falls).

### Scenario: Creating a Travel Vlog Title

Imagine you are editing a travel vlog. You have a beautiful, sweeping shot of a beach, and you want to introduce the location. 

1. You move the playhead to the start of the beach clip and add a text box reading "Maui, Hawaii".
2. You select a clean, bold font to convey a modern travel aesthetic. 
3. You change the text color to a bright, tropical blue, matching the ocean in your shot.
4. Because the sky and ocean are bright, you add a thin, white outline to ensure the dark blue text doesn't vanish into the background.
5. Finally, you add a subtle drop shadow to give the title a slight 3D effect, making it look professionally placed rather than flatly stamped onto the video.

## Using Auto Captions

Manually typing out text for titles or labels is manageable when you only have a few words. But if you are creating a tutorial, a podcast clip, or an interview, typing out every spoken word as a subtitle is incredibly time-consuming. 

This is where CapCut’s **Auto Captions** feature becomes invaluable. Using AI, CapCut listens to the audio track of your video and automatically transcribes the spoken words into text, placing them on the Timeline in sync with the audio.

### Why Use Auto Captions?

A massive percentage of video content today is consumed on mobile devices with the sound muted. If your video relies entirely on audio to convey its message, you risk losing a large portion of your audience. Auto captions solve this problem instantly. Furthermore, captions are essential for accessibility, allowing viewers who are deaf or hard of hearing to engage with your content.

### Generating Auto Captions

Because this feature relies on analyzing your audio, it is crucial that your audio is clear. If you followed the steps in *Audio Integration and Sound Design* to ensure your voiceover or dialogue is crisp, the AI will have a much easier time transcribing it accurately.

1. Tap the **Text** icon from the main bottom menu.
2. Select **Auto captions**.
3. CapCut will analyze the audio on your Timeline. 
4. You may be prompted to select the source audio (usually the main video track or a specific voiceover track) and the spoken language.
5. Tap **Start**.

Once the process is complete, CapCut will generate a new **Text Track** on your Timeline. However, instead of one long text clip, you will see dozens of short text clips, each representing a sentence or phrase. CapCut automatically times these clips to appear and disappear exactly when those words are spoken.

### Reviewing and Editing Auto Captions

While AI transcription is remarkably accurate, it is not perfect. It can misinterpret industry jargon, names, or words spoken with heavy accents. Therefore, treating auto-generated captions as a first draft rather than a final product is essential.

To edit the transcribed text:
1. Tap the specific text clip on the Timeline that contains an error.
2. Tap the **Edit** (pencil) icon.
3. Use your keyboard to correct the spelling or phrasing, just as you would edit a normal text box.
4. Tap **Done**.

You can also adjust the duration of individual caption clips if the AI cut a word off too early or too late. Simply drag the edges of the specific caption clip on the Timeline to fine-tune its timing.

### Styling Auto Captions

When you generate auto captions, CapCut applies a default text style to all of them. If you want to change the font, color, or size of your captions, you do not have to edit every single clip individually. 

1. Tap on any one of the auto-caption clips on your Timeline.
2. Tap the **Style** option in the bottom menu.
3. Choose your desired font, color, outline, and background.
4. CapCut will ask if you want to apply these changes to all captions in the project. Confirm this action.

Within seconds, every single caption clip across your entire video will update to match your new style. This batch-editing capability saves immense amounts of time and ensures visual consistency throughout your video.

## Applying Text Animations and Tracking

Static text is functional, but adding movement makes your titles visually engaging. In video editing, applying movement to the appearance or disappearance of text is called **Text Animation**. 

*Note: In this chapter, we are focusing strictly on animations that apply to text elements. We will cover broader video transitions and animations in a later chapter.*

### Text Animations

CapCut divides text animations into two categories: **In** animations and **Out** animations. 

*   **In Animations:** Dictate how the text enters the screen. For example, you can make the text fade in, slide in from the left, or pop up in the center.
*   **Out Animations:** Dictate how the text exits the screen. You can make it fade out, slide off to the right, or shrink away.

To apply an animation:
1. Select your text clip on the Timeline.
2. Tap the **Animation** icon in the bottom menu.
3. Browse through the **In** and **Out** tabs to preview the different effects.
4. Tap an effect to apply it.

When you apply an animation, a small colored bar will appear at the beginning (for In animations) or end (for Out animations) of your text clip on the Timeline. This bar represents the **duration** of the animation—the time it takes for the text to fully enter or exit the screen. You can drag the edges of this colored bar to make the animation happen faster or slower. A longer duration creates a slow, dramatic fade, while a shorter duration creates a snappy, energetic pop.

### Text Tracking

As you add more text to your videos, you may want to reuse specific styles without having to remember your exact font, color, and size settings. CapCut solves this with a feature called **Tracking**.

In design and video editing, tracking refers to the uniform spacing between characters in a block of text. However, in the context of CapCut's text tools, "tracking" also relates to saving and managing your text styles. 

When you perfect a text style—say, a specific bold yellow font with a black outline for your channel's branding—you can save it. By selecting the styled text and choosing the **Save** or **Add to favorites** option in the style menu, CapCut remembers those exact parameters. 

The next time you add a new text box, you can go to your saved styles, tap your saved preset, and instantly apply the exact same look. This ensures that if you are adding multiple titles throughout a long video, your branding remains perfectly consistent without requiring you to manually input the settings every single time.

## Key Takeaways

*   Text in CapCut exists on its own dedicated track, allowing it to hover above video and audio without altering the original media.
*   You can modify a text clip’s duration by manually dragging its edges on the Timeline or by using the precise Duration tool for exact timing.
*   Customizing text involves adjusting fonts, sizes, colors, and utilizing outlines and shadows to ensure readability against any background.
*   The Auto Captions feature uses AI to automatically transcribe spoken audio into text, saving hours of manual typing.
*   Auto captions are a first draft; always review and edit them for spelling and timing accuracy.
*   You can batch-edit the style of all auto captions simultaneously, ensuring visual consistency and saving time.
*   Text animations (In and Out) control how text enters and exits the screen, adding dynamic movement to your titles.
*   Saving your favorite text styles allows you to quickly apply consistent branding across multiple text boxes in your project.

modules.title: Transitions and Basic Animations
modules.content:
Imagine watching a movie where every single scene change is a hard, abrupt cut. The actor is mid-sentence in a bustling coffee shop, and instantly, they are standing in a silent forest. While straight cuts are the backbone of video editing—as you learned in Core Cutting and Sequencing—stringing too many of them together without a visual break can leave your audience feeling disoriented and fatigued. 

This is where transitions and animations step in. They are the connective tissue of your video. A well-placed fade can indicate the passage of time, a playful slide can match the energy of a background music track, and a simple pop-in animation can draw the viewer’s eye to a crucial piece of text. 

By the end of this chapter, you will understand how to breathe life into your static clips, text, and stickers, transforming a simple sequence of video clips into a dynamic, engaging story.

## Transitions vs. Animations: What is the Difference?

Before we open CapCut and start applying effects, it is vital to understand the difference between a transition and an animation. In everyday conversation, people often use these words interchangeably. In video editing, however, they refer to two distinctly different mechanics.

**Transitions** live *between* two clips. They affect the seam where Clip A ends and Clip B begins. When you apply a transition, you are telling CapCut how to blend, slide, or wipe the outgoing clip into the incoming clip. Transitions require at least two pieces of media to function, and they always happen at the cut point.

**Animations** live *on* a single clip or element. They dictate how that specific item enters the screen, moves while it is on the screen, or exits the screen. You can apply an animation to a single, isolated video clip, a piece of text, or a sticker. Animations do not require a second clip to function.

Think of it like a theater stage. A transition is how the curtain falls and rises to change the scenery between acts. An animation is how an actor walks onto the stage, strikes a pose, and then walks off.

## Applying Transitions Between Clips

CapCut offers a vast library of transitions, ranging from simple and professional to flashy and trendy. To keep things organized, they are generally grouped into a few categories:

*   **Basic:** Includes the most fundamental and widely used transitions, such as fades, slides, and wipes.
*   **Overlay:** Transitions that use light or color blending, such as a flash or a light leak.
*   **Trendy:** More complex, stylized transitions designed to grab attention (often used in social media content).

### The Anatomy of a Transition

When you add a transition, CapCut creates a small overlapping zone between Clip A and Clip B. 

Imagine Clip A is 5 seconds long, and Clip B is 5 seconds long. If you add a 1-second transition between them, CapCut essentially overlaps the last 0.5 seconds of Clip A with the first 0.5 seconds of Clip B. The total length of your timeline will shrink by 1 second (from 10 seconds down to 9 seconds), because those two half-seconds are now playing simultaneously as a blended transition.

### How to Apply a Transition

Let’s walk through the process of adding a transition to your Main Track.

1. Open your project in CapCut and ensure you have at least two clips placed sequentially on your timeline.
2. Look closely at the timeline. Right at the junction where Clip A meets Clip B, you will see a small, white rectangular block with a vertical line through it. This is the **Transition Button**.
3. Tap the Transition Button. 
4. The Transition menu will open at the bottom of your screen. Scroll horizontally to browse the categories (Basic, Overlay, Trendy, etc.).
5. Tap on any transition icon to preview it. The preview will automatically loop on your main screen.
6. Once you find one you like, tap the **Checkmark** in the bottom right corner to apply it.

### Adjusting Transition Duration and Overlap

Sometimes a transition feels too slow and sluggish, or too fast and jarring. Fortunately, you have precise control over how long the transition lasts.

1. Tap on the transition icon you just placed between your clips on the timeline.
2. The Transition menu will reappear. Look for the **Duration** slider or input box.
3. Drag the slider left to decrease the time (making the transition faster) or right to increase the time (making it slower). You can also type in a specific duration, such as 0.3s or 1.5s.
4. Tap the Play button to preview the adjusted speed.
5. Tap the Checkmark to save your changes.

**A note on duration:** For most standard cuts, a transition duration of 0.3 to 0.5 seconds feels natural. If a transition lasts longer than 1 or 2 seconds, it risks distracting the viewer from the actual content of the video.

### Essential Transitions to Know

While CapCut frequently updates its library with new effects, three basic transitions form the foundation of video editing. Master these first.

**1. Fade to Black**
This is arguably the most important transition in filmmaking. The outgoing clip slowly darkens to a black screen, and then the incoming clip slowly brightens up from the black screen. 
*   **When to use it:** Use a fade to black to signal a major shift in time, a change of location, or the end of a specific "chapter" in your video. It tells the audience, "That part is over; we are starting something new."

**2. Cross Dissolve (or Mix)**
Instead of fading to black, Clip A slowly becomes transparent while Clip B slowly becomes opaque simultaneously. The two images blend together for a moment.
*   **When to use it:** Dissolves are softer than fades to black. They are perfect for showing the passage of a short amount of time, or for linking two clips that share a thematic connection (e.g., dissolving from a close-up of a person’s face to a close-up of what they are looking at).

**3. Slides and Wipes**
In a slide transition, Clip A pushes physically to the left, right, up, or down, revealing Clip B underneath it. In a wipe, a line moves across the screen, wiping away Clip A to reveal Clip B.
*   **When to use it:** These are energetic and fast-paced. They are highly popular in vlogs, travel videos, and social media content. Use them to keep momentum going between similar shots, like moving from a shot of a plane taking off to a shot of a beach.

## Applying In-and-Out Animations

Now that we have covered how to bridge the gap *between* clips, let’s look at how to animate the clips and elements themselves. 

In CapCut, animations are divided into three categories based on when they occur:
*   **In:** How the element enters the screen. (e.g., fading in, sliding in from the left, popping up).
*   **Out:** How the element exits the screen. (e.g., fading out, sliding down, zooming away).
*   **Loop:** A continuous movement that plays for as long as the element is on the screen. (e.g., pulsing, vibrating, or slowly zooming in).

For this chapter, we will focus on "In" and "Out" animations, as looping animations often involve keyframing, which we will cover in a later module.

### Animating Video Clips

You can apply animations to any video clip on your timeline, whether it is on the Main Track or an overlay track. 

1. Tap on a video clip in your timeline to select it. The clip will be highlighted.
2. In the bottom toolbar, scroll horizontally until you find the **Animation** button and tap it.
3. You will see three tabs: **In**, **Out**, and **Loop**.
4. Tap the **In** tab. Browse the animation options (such as Fade In, Slide Right, Zoom In). Tap an icon to preview it.
5. Once selected, use the **Duration** slider at the bottom to dictate how long the entrance animation lasts (usually between 0.1s and 2.0s).
6. Tap the Checkmark to apply.
7. Repeat this process using the **Out** tab if you want the clip to animate as it leaves the screen.

**How to read animation on the timeline:** When you apply an "In" or "Out" animation to a clip, CapCut places a small, colored triangle on the clip block on your timeline. An "In" animation places a triangle at the very beginning of the clip, while an "Out" animation places a triangle at the very end. If you ever want to remove an animation, simply tap the clip, tap Animation, and select the blank "None" icon at the beginning of the options.

### Animating Text and Stickers

Animations are not just for video. In fact, they are arguably most useful for the Text and Stickers you learned about in previous chapters. Because text and stickers are usually static graphics, adding motion to them prevents them from looking like flat, boring stickers slapped onto your footage.

The process for animating text and stickers is identical to animating video clips:

1. Add your text or sticker to the timeline.
2. Tap the text layer or sticker layer to select it.
3. Tap the **Animation** button in the toolbar.
4. Choose an **In** animation (e.g., "Typewriter" for text, "Pop" for a sticker) and adjust the duration.
5. Choose an **Out** animation so the element leaves the screen gracefully rather than just disappearing abruptly.

**A realistic scenario:** Imagine you are making a short promotional video for a local bakery. You have a clip of a barista pouring a latte. You add text that says "Freshly Brewed." If the text just appears on screen, it might look out of place. But if you apply an "In" animation where the text slides up from the bottom over 0.5 seconds, and an "Out" animation where it fades out over 0.5 seconds, the text suddenly feels integrated into the motion of the video. 

## Combining Transitions and Animations

Understanding the difference between transitions and animations allows you to use them together strategically to create professional-looking sequences. Because transitions affect the space *between* clips and animations affect the clips *themselves*, they do not cancel each other out.

Here are a few ways to combine them effectively:

*   **The Seamless Flow:** Apply a "Slide Left" transition between Clip A and Clip B. Then, apply a "Slide Left" In-animation to a piece of text on Clip B. As the video physically slides to reveal Clip B, the text slides in at the exact same speed and direction. This creates a highly synchronized, professional effect.
*   **The Emphasis Cut:** Use a "Flash" transition between two clips to create a high-energy burst of light. Immediately after the flash, use a "Zoom In" In-animation on the incoming clip. The flash masks the cut, and the zoom makes the new clip feel like it is aggressively jumping toward the viewer. This is perfect for matching action to a heavy drum beat in your audio track.
*   **The Soft Handoff:** Apply a "Cross Dissolve" transition between two clips. Add a "Fade In" In-animation to a sticker on the second clip, and a "Fade Out" Out-animation to a sticker on the first clip. The video, the transition, and the graphic elements all melt into one another smoothly.

### A Word on Restraint

When you first discover the vast libraries of transitions and animations in CapCut, it is incredibly tempting to use a different, flashy effect on every single cut. This is a common trap for beginner editors. 

If every clip zooms, every text pops, and every transition is a dazzling light show, the video becomes chaotic. The audience will quickly experience sensory overload, and the actual message of your video will be lost in the visual noise. 

Professional editors rely heavily on straight cuts and simple dissolves, saving flashy transitions and animations for moments that truly deserve emphasis. Use motion to guide your viewer's eye and enhance your story, not just to show off an effect. When in doubt, leave it out.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Transitions** live in the space *between* two clips, dictating how Clip A blends into Clip B. They require at least two pieces of media.
*   **Animations** live *on* a single clip, text, or sticker, dictating how that element enters (**In**) or exits (**Out**) the screen.
*   To add a transition, tap the white Transition Button located at the junction between two clips on your timeline.
*   Transition duration controls the overlap of the two clips. A 1-second transition overlaps 0.5 seconds of the outgoing clip with 0.5 seconds of the incoming clip.
*   **Fade to Black** signals a major shift in time or story; **Cross Dissolve** indicates a gentle passage of time; **Slides and Wipes** provide high-energy momentum.
*   You can animate video clips, text, and stickers using the **Animation** button in the toolbar. Look for the colored triangles on the timeline to know where animations are applied.
*   Transitions and animations can be combined to create synchronized, professional-looking visual effects, but they should be used with restraint to avoid overwhelming the viewer.

modules.title: Overlays, Stickers, and Effects
modules.content:
Imagine you are editing a video of a friend opening a much-anticipated gift. You have the main video of them tearing off the wrapping paper, but you want to make the moment more cinematic. You want to add a subtle glowing light leak over the footage to give it a dreamy atmosphere. You also want to place a small video clip of a ticking clock in the corner of the screen to build suspense. And finally, right when they open the box, you want a bright, animated "Wow!" emoji to pop up on screen. 

To accomplish any of this, you need to break free from editing on a single layer. You need to learn how to stack elements on top of one another. 

In the earlier chapters on Core Cutting and Sequencing and Text, Titles, and Auto Captions, you spent all your time on the **main track**—the foundational layer of your Timeline where your primary video clips live. Now, we are going to look upward. In this chapter, you will learn how to use overlay tracks to stack videos and images, mix them together using blend modes, and inject instant personality into your project with stickers and visual effects.

## Understanding the Overlay Track

If you have ever used image editing software, you are likely familiar with the concept of layers. Think of video editing in CapCut the exact same way. 

Your main track is the bottom sheet of paper. It is the background. An **overlay track** is a transparent sheet of plastic that you place over that background. You can draw or place a video on that transparent sheet, and the background video will still show through wherever the overlay isn't covering it. 

CapCut allows you to add multiple overlay tracks above your main track. This means you can have your main background video, a secondary video playing in a smaller box in the corner, a graphic logo in the opposite corner, and a text layer, all visible at the exact same time.

### Adding an Overlay

To add an overlay track, you do not drag media onto your existing Timeline. Instead, you use a dedicated menu.

1. Open your project in CapCut.
2. Move your **Playhead** to the exact moment where you want the overlay to appear.
3. Tap the **Overlay** icon in the main toolbar (usually located at the bottom of the screen, beneath the Timeline).
4. Tap **Add Overlay**.
5. Your device’s media gallery will open. Select the video or image you want to stack, then tap **Add**.
