Once you do this, you will notice a new track appears *above* your main video track. This is your overlay track. The clip you selected will rest there. 

### Manipulating Overlay Clips

Overlay clips behave exactly like your main track clips. You can use the same trimming techniques you learned in Core Cutting and Sequencing to shorten or extend them. You can tap and drag the edges to change the duration, and you can tap the clip and select **Split** to cut it into pieces.

However, because overlays sit on top of the background, they have one special property: you can move and resize them on the preview screen.

When you tap an overlay clip in the Timeline, a bounding box will appear around it in the video preview window. 
* **To move it:** Place your finger inside the box and drag it to a new position on the screen.
* **To resize it:** Pinch inward with two fingers to make the overlay smaller, or spread two fingers apart to make it larger. 
* **To rotate it:** Place two fingers on the overlay and twist them left or right.

**A Practical Scenario: The Picture-in-Picture Effect**
One of the most common uses for overlays is the Picture-in-Picture (PiP) effect. Imagine you are making a reaction video. Your main track is a clip from a viral video. You add an overlay track containing a video of yourself watching that video. You resize your reaction video to fit in the bottom right corner of the screen, and suddenly, the audience can see both the original video and your reaction simultaneously. 

## Blend Modes: Mixing Layers Together

Simply placing an overlay on top of a background is useful, but what if you want the two videos to interact with one another? What if you want to create a double-exposure effect, where two videos melt into each other? This is where blend modes come in.

A **blend mode** is a setting that tells CapCut how the pixels of the overlay clip should mathematically mix with the pixels of the clip directly beneath it. Instead of the overlay just sitting *on top* of the background, the two layers combine to create a brand-new look.

### Accessing Blend Modes

To access blend modes, tap your overlay clip in the Timeline. In the toolbar beneath the Timeline, scroll horizontally until you find the **Blend** option and tap it. 

You will be presented with a list of options grouped into three main categories: **Darken**, **Lighten**, and **Overlay** (sometimes referred to as Contrast). Let’s look at how the most common ones work.

*   **Multiply (Darken category):** This mode looks at the color information in both layers and multiplies them. In practical terms, it makes the overlay transparent to pure white, but keeps all the dark and colored areas. This is incredibly useful for adding black-and-white graphics, like a film grain texture or a paper border, over your video. The white background of the graphic disappears, leaving only the black texture behind.
*   **Screen (Lighten category):** This is the exact opposite of Multiply. It makes pure black transparent and keeps the light areas. If you have a video of a glowing sparkler or fireworks shot against a pitch-black background, using the Screen blend mode will make the black disappear, leaving only the glowing sparks floating magically over your main video.
*   **Overlay (Contrast category):** This mode mixes the two clips by keeping the darks dark and the lights light, but blending the mid-tones. It is frequently used to blend a texture—like falling rain, light leaks, or dust particles—over a video so that the texture becomes a part of the original footage.

**A Practical Scenario: Adding a Light Leak**
You downloaded a video clip of a cinematic "light leak"—a beautiful swirl of orange and red light moving across a black screen. If you just place it as an overlay, you will see a black box with orange light moving over your main video. 
To fix this, tap the overlay, select **Blend**, and choose **Screen**. Instantly, the black background vanishes. The orange and red light will appear to bleed beautifully across your underlying footage, giving your video a vintage, film-camera feel.

## Adding Stickers and Emojis

While overlays are for stacking your own media, CapCut also features a massive built-in library of animated graphics. **Stickers** are pre-made graphics, emojis, and animations that you can drop onto your video to add flair, humor, or context. 

Unlike text, which we covered in Chapter 5, stickers are visual elements. They are added to their own specialized overlay tracks.

### Inserting a Sticker

1. Move your Playhead to where you want the sticker to appear.
2. Tap the **Stickers** icon in the main toolbar.
3. A menu will pop up showing various categories. 
4. Browse the categories (which include emojis, arrows, social media icons, and seasonal graphics) or use the search bar to find something specific.
5. Tap a sticker to add it to your video.

Once added, the sticker will appear on a new track in your Timeline, and a bounding box will appear around it in the preview window. Just like video overlays, you can pinch, drag, and rotate the sticker to place it exactly where you want.

### Adjusting Sticker Timing

Stickers usually have a default duration applied when you add them. If you want an emoji to pop up on screen for exactly two seconds when someone says something surprising, you simply tap the sticker clip in the Timeline and drag its right edge inward to shorten it to the desired length. 

CapCut also offers interactive stickers, like **Polls** or **Q&A** boxes, which are highly useful if you are editing content specifically for TikTok. When viewers see these stickers, they can actually tap them to interact with your video.

## Applying Visual Effects

While overlays and stickers add specific elements to your screen, **Effects** (often called Visual Effects or VFX) alter the entire look of your footage. 

If you want to simulate an earthquake, make it look like it's raining, give your video a VHS tape glitch, or add a dreamy glow, you use effects. CapCut separates effects into two distinct categories: **Video Effects** (which apply to the whole project or specific clips) and **Body Effects** (which track a person's movement). For now, we will focus on Video Effects.

### Adding a Video Effect

1. Tap the **Effects** icon in the main toolbar.
2. Select **Video Effects**.
3. Browse the categories (such as Retro, Comic, Night, or Lens).
4. Tap any effect to see a preview of what it will do.
5. Tap the checkmark or **Add** button to apply it.

When you add a Video Effect, it creates a new effect layer on your Timeline. By default, this effect spans the entire length of your video. 

### Controlling Effect Duration

If you only want an effect to play for a short burst—say, a glitch effect that lasts for half a second during a scene change—you need to adjust its duration. 

Tap the effect layer in the Timeline. Just like a video clip, it has handles on the left and right sides. Drag the right handle to the left to shorten the effect. You can also tap and drag the entire effect layer to move it to a different part of your video without changing its length.

### Adjusting Effect Intensity

Sometimes, an effect is simply too strong. A glitch effect might make your video unwatchable, or a glow effect might wash out your subjects' faces. 

To fix this, tap the effect layer in your Timeline. In the toolbar, tap **Adjust** (or the settings icon). Here, you will usually find a slider for **Intensity**. Dragging this slider to the left decreases the strength of the effect, allowing you to find a subtle, balanced look. Some effects also have additional sliders here, letting you adjust parameters like speed, size, or color.

**A Practical Scenario: The Earthquake Effect**
You are editing an action video where a character jumps and lands on the ground. You want the screen to shake when they land. 
1. Move your Playhead to the exact frame where they hit the ground.
2. Go to **Effects** > **Video Effects** and search for **Shake** or **Earthquake**.
3. Add it to your project. 
4. Because the effect covers your whole Timeline, tap the effect layer and drag its left edge so it begins exactly at the moment of impact. 
5. Drag the right edge so the effect ends about one second later. 
6. Tap the effect, select **Adjust**, and lower the intensity so it feels like a natural tremor rather than a chaotic blur.

## A Word on Filters vs. Effects

As you explore CapCut, you will notice a **Filters** option sitting right next to the Effects option in the toolbar. It is important to understand the difference, especially since Chapter 8 is dedicated entirely to Color Correction and Adjustment.

*   **Effects** are dramatic visual changes. They add moving elements, distort the image, or overlay new graphics (like rain, glitches, or light beams). 
*   **Filters** are preset color grades. They adjust the brightness, contrast, and color tones of your video to create a specific mood (like a "Cool" blue filter or a "Warm" golden-hour filter). 

While you can use Filters right now to quickly change the mood of your video, we will hold off on diving deep into them. For the rest of this chapter, focus on using Effects to add visual flair, and save the fine-tuning of colors for Chapter 8.

## Structuring a Complex Timeline

As you add overlays, stickers, and effects, your Timeline will start to look crowded. A single project might have a main video track, an overlay for a logo, a sticker for an emoji, an effect layer for a glitch, and a text layer for a caption. 

Keeping track of all these moving parts is a skill in itself. Here are a few tips for managing a complex Timeline:

*   **Rename Your Tracks (If on Desktop/Advanced Interface):** If you are using a version of CapCut that allows track naming, give your tracks descriptive names like "Logo Overlay" or "Rain Effect." This saves you from having to tap every clip to figure out what it is.
*   **Use the Visibility Toggle:** In CapCut, you can temporarily hide a track to see what is beneath it or to focus on editing one specific layer. Tapping the eye icon next to a track will hide it from the preview window without deleting it.
*   **Layer Order Matters:** The order of your tracks from top to bottom dictates what is on top. If your text is being hidden by an overlay, it means the overlay track is above the text track. You can usually select a clip and use an option like "Bring to Front" or drag the clip to a higher track to fix the layering.

## Combining Tools: A Mini-Project

To see how all these elements work together, let’s walk through a quick scenario combining overlays, blend modes, stickers, and effects.

Imagine you are making a short, 10-second video for social media. The main footage is a shot of your friend walking down the street looking bored. 

1.  **The Main Track:** You trim the footage of your friend walking so it lasts exactly 10 seconds.
2.  **The Overlay (Blend Mode):** You want to make it look like it's raining. You import a video of a rain overlay you downloaded. You add it as an Overlay. You tap the overlay, go to **Blend**, and select **Screen**. The black background of the rain video disappears, and now it looks like rain is falling over your friend. You adjust the size so the rain covers the whole screen.
3.  **The Sticker:** To add some humor, you go to **Stickers** and search for a thought bubble. You place it above your friend's head and resize it to fit the screen. You shorten the sticker clip so it only appears from second 3 to second 7.
4.  **The Effect:** At the very end of the video, when your friend looks directly at the camera, you want a dramatic zoom. You go to **Video Effects**, find the **Zoom** effect, and add it. You drag the effect layer so it only covers the final two seconds of the video. You tap the effect, go to **Adjust**, and set the speed to fast.

In just a few minutes, you have taken a single, boring clip and transformed it into a dynamic, multi-layered story using four different tracks. This is the true power of overlays, stickers, and effects. They allow you to build a world around your main footage, guiding the viewer's eye and setting the exact tone you want.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Overlays** are tracks placed above the main video track, allowing you to stack videos, images, and graphics on top of the background footage.
*   Overlay clips can be moved, resized, and rotated directly in the preview window using pinch and drag gestures.
*   **Blend modes** (like Multiply and Screen) mathematically mix the overlay layer with the background layer, allowing you to remove black or white backgrounds from overlay clips to create seamless integrations.
*   **Stickers** are pre-made, animated graphics and emojis found in CapCut’s built-in library. They are added to specialized overlay tracks.
*   **Video Effects** alter the overall look of the footage by adding elements like glitches, shakes, or glows. They appear as their own layers on the Timeline.
*   Effect layers can be trimmed, moved, and adjusted in intensity to ensure they enhance, rather than overpower, the main video.
*   **Filters** are different from Effects; they adjust color and mood, a topic that will be covered in depth in Chapter 8.

modules.title: Color Correction and Adjustment
modules.content:
Have you ever filmed a beautiful sunset, only to look at the footage later and find the sky looks washed out and gray? Or perhaps you recorded an indoor vlog, and your skin looks strangely orange and shadowy. This is a common frustration: the human eye is incredibly good at adjusting to different lighting, but camera sensors simply record what is in front of them. 

The good news is that raw footage is rarely a final destination. By using CapCut’s color adjustment tools, you can rescue flat, dull, or poorly lit clips and transform them into cinematic, visually striking shots. Color correction is the secret sauce that separates amateur-looking videos from professional ones. 

## The Building Blocks of Color: Luma and Chroma

Before we start dragging sliders, we need to define a few terms. In video editing, we generally divide an image into two categories: **Luma** and **Chroma**.

-   **Luma** refers to the brightness (luminance) of your image. It is the black-and-white structural foundation of your video. When you adjust the Luma, you are changing how light or dark the shot is, without affecting the actual colors. 
-   **Chroma** refers to the color (chrominance) information. This includes how intense the colors are and what specific hues they are. 

Color correction is the process of fixing the Luma and Chroma so the video looks natural, balanced, and realistic. Color grading (which we will touch on using filters) is the creative step of altering those colors to create a specific mood or aesthetic. 

## Accessing the Adjustment Tools

To begin modifying your video's visual properties, you first need to access the adjustment panel. 

1. Open your existing project in CapCut, or start a **New Project** and add a clip to your **Timeline**.
2. Tap once on the video clip in the **Timeline** to select it. A white border will appear around the clip, and the main toolbar menu at the bottom of the screen will update.
3. Scroll through the bottom toolbar until you find the **Adjust** button and tap it. 

This opens the Adjust panel, which contains a list of sliders. You will see options like Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Exposure, and more. At the top of the screen, your video preview remains visible so you can see your changes in real-time. 

## Modifying Brightness, Contrast, and Saturation

The holy trinity of basic color correction is Brightness, Contrast, and Saturation. Mastering these three sliders will fix about 90% of common video issues.

### Brightness
**Brightness** controls the overall lightness or darkness of your entire image. If your footage was shot in a dimly lit room and is hard to see, increasing the Brightness will lift the shadows and make the whole frame lighter. If your footage is blindingly bright (overexposed), decreasing the Brightness will bring it back to a viewable level.

*Scenario:* You filmed a video diary in your bedroom at night, with only a single desk lamp. The camera struggled to capture your face, leaving you in heavy shadow. By nudging the Brightness slider up by +15, your face becomes visible. However, pushing it too high will make the image look gray and washed out, introducing visual noise (grain). 

### Contrast
**Contrast** is the difference between the darkest and lightest parts of your image. A low-contrast image looks flat, hazy, and gray. A high-contrast image looks punchy, with deep blacks and bright whites. 

After you increase the Brightness of a dark shot, the image often looks flat. This is where Contrast comes in. By increasing the Contrast slightly, you put the depth back into the image. The dark areas (like the shadows in the corners of your room) become properly dark again, while the lit areas (like your face) retain their new brightness.

### Saturation
**Saturation** dictates the intensity of your colors. A saturation level of zero results in a pure black-and-white video. High saturation makes colors vivid and vibrant, while low saturation makes them muted and pastel.

Cameras often shoot slightly dull footage by default to preserve detail. Increasing the Saturation by +10 or +15 can make a green forest look lush and a blue sky look striking. However, be careful not to overdo it. Pushing saturation too high makes skin tones look unnaturally red or orange, a common mistake that immediately signals an amateur edit.

## Expanding Your Toolkit: Exposure, Highlights, and Shadows

While Brightness, Contrast, and Saturation are your primary tools, CapCut offers a few more Luma-based sliders that give you much finer control.

-   **Exposure:** Similar to Brightness, but it mimics how a camera lens lets in light. Increasing exposure brightens the image but does so more aggressively than the Brightness slider. 
-   **Highlights:** This slider *only* affects the brightest parts of your image. If you film outdoors and the sky is a blinding white blob, decreasing the Highlights can bring back detail in the clouds without darkening the rest of your scene.
-   **Shadows:** This slider *only* affects the darkest parts of your image. If someone’s face is hidden in a dark shadow, increasing the Shadows slider will lighten those specific dark areas without blowing out the bright sky behind them. 
-   **Temperature:** This moves your colors between blue (cool) and orange/yellow (warm). Indoor lighting often makes video look too orange; sliding the Temperature slightly toward blue fixes this. 
-   **Tint:** This moves your colors between green and magenta. It is rarely needed, but useful if your camera produces a strange green color cast under fluorescent lights.

## Applying Color Filters for Mood and Aesthetic

Once you have corrected your footage so it looks natural and balanced—meaning the blacks are deep, the whites are clean, and the colors are realistic—you can move on to color grading. 

Color grading is where you inject emotion. A horror video feels cold and blue. A nostalgic memory feels warm and slightly faded. A sci-fi tech video might feel high-contrast and green. In CapCut, the easiest way to apply these aesthetics is by using **Filters**.

### How to Apply a Filter
1. Deselect your clip by tapping anywhere in the empty timeline space, or simply navigate to the main bottom toolbar.
2. Tap the **Filters** button (usually located near the Adjust button).
3. You will be presented with a grid of categories (e.g., Portrait, Food, Scenery, Film, Retro). 
4. Tap a category, then tap a specific filter thumbnail. The preview will instantly update to show you what that filter looks like.

### Adjusting Filter Intensity
Filters in CapCut are applied at 100% strength by default, which is often far too strong. A heavy filter can crush the details in your footage and make it look artificial. 

When you select a filter, a slider appears at the bottom of the screen. This controls the **Intensity** of the filter. A great practice for beginners is to choose the filter you like, and then drag the Intensity slider down to somewhere between 30% and 50%. This applies the aesthetic mood of the filter while still letting the natural details of your footage shine through.

## Skin Tone Enhancement and Sharpening

Two of the most important subjects in any video are people's faces and the crispness of the details. CapCut includes specific tools to help enhance both.

### Skin Tone Enhancement
When you are in the **Adjust** panel, you will notice a slider specifically labeled **Skin** (sometimes updated in app versions as "Skin Tone"). 

This slider is designed to analyze the Luma and Chroma of human faces and optimize them. Sliding it to the right generally warms up the skin, smooths out harsh redness, and makes faces look healthier and more evenly lit. Sliding it to the left does the opposite. 

If you are filming a "talking head" vlog, an interview, or a tutorial where you are speaking directly to the camera, a slight bump to the Skin slider (+5 to +10) can make your complexion look professionally lit, even if you just filmed in your living room. 

### Sharpening
Digital video footage, especially when compressed by smartphones, can sometimes look slightly soft or slightly blurry around the edges. **Sharpening** is a tool that increases the contrast along the edges of objects in your frame, making the image look crisper.

In the Adjust panel, find the **Sharpen** slider. Sliding it to the right will make textures—like hair, fabric, and leaves—pop. However, sharpening is a classic case of "less is more." 

If you push the Sharpen slider too high, the video will start to look gritty. You will see "halos" or glowing outlines around high-contrast edges, like where a dark tree branch meets a bright sky. For most footage, a subtle sharpening value of +10 to +20 is plenty to make the video look crisp and professional without introducing ugly artifacts.

## Copying and Pasting Adjustments for Consistency

Imagine you have just spent five minutes perfectly color-correcting a single clip in your **Timeline**. You balanced the exposure, fixed the temperature, and added a slight sharpen. But your sequence has ten more clips, all shot in the exact same room with the exact same lighting. Do you have to repeat all those steps ten times?

Thankfully, no. CapCut allows you to copy your adjustment attributes from one clip and paste them to others. This ensures visual consistency across your entire video, which is a hallmark of professional editing. (Jumping colors wildly from clip to clip is jarring to the viewer).

### Step-by-Step: Copying Attributes
1. Tap the clip in your Timeline that already has the perfect adjustments applied to it.
2. In the bottom toolbar, scroll until you find the **Copy** icon. Tap it. (This duplicates the entire clip on your timeline, which is helpful, but not what we want right now).
3. Right next to the Copy icon, you will see an icon that looks like two overlapping squares or a clipboard. This is the **Paste Attributes** (or sometimes just "Paste") button. Tap it.
4. A menu will pop up asking exactly what you want to paste. CapCut lets you choose to paste just the Adjustments, just the Filters, or both. Select **Adjust** (and Filters if you want to copy the aesthetic look too).
5. The adjustments will instantly be applied to the very next clip on your timeline.

### Applying to Multiple Clips
If you want to apply the adjustments to a sequence of several clips at once, CapCut makes this easy:

1. Ensure your perfectly corrected clip is on the timeline.
2. Tap the **Paste Attributes** icon.
3. When the menu pops up, look for an option that says **Apply to All** or use the multi-select tool (usually an icon of multiple stacked rectangles) to highlight all the clips in your timeline that you want to affect.
4. Confirm the paste. 

In an instant, all selected clips will inherit the exact same Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, and Sharpen values. This is incredibly powerful for maintaining a uniform look, especially when you assembled your video using the techniques from *Core Cutting and Sequencing*. 

## A Quick Color Correction Workflow

When you sit down to color correct your video, the order in which you apply your adjustments matters. If you add saturation to a clip that is too dark, you will just amplify ugly visual noise. Here is a reliable, step-by-step workflow for any clip:

1. **Fix the Luma first:** Open the Adjust panel and fix the overall light. Start with Brightness or Exposure to get the shot to a watchable level. 
2. **Refine the Luma:** Use the Highlights and Shadows sliders to recover details. Bring down blindingly bright skies, and lift deep, dark shadows hiding your subject's face.
3. **Add Contrast:** Once the light is balanced, increase the Contrast to remove the flat, hazy look and give the image depth.
4. **Fix the Chroma:** Adjust the Temperature to fix any orange or blue color casts from indoor or outdoor lighting. 
5. **Enhance Saturation:** Bump up the Saturation slightly to make the colors pop, and use the Skin slider to ensure any human faces look natural and healthy.
6. **Sharpen:** Apply a small amount of Sharpening to make the edges crisp.
7. **Filter (Optional):** Exit the Adjust panel, open the Filters panel, and apply a mood filter, remembering to lower the Intensity so it doesn't overpower your work.
8. **Copy:** Use Paste Attributes to copy this exact setup to the rest of the clips shot in that same environment.

## Key Takeaways
- **Luma** controls brightness, while **Chroma** controls color. Fix the Luma first, then refine the Chroma.
- **Brightness, Contrast, and Saturation** are your primary tools. Brightness fixes overall light, Contrast adds depth by separating darks and lights, and Saturation dictates color intensity.
- **Highlights and Shadows** allow for targeted Luma adjustments, letting you fix bright skies and dark faces independently.
- **Filters** are used for color grading—applying a creative mood or aesthetic. Always lower the filter Intensity (often to 30-50%) to keep the video looking natural.
- **Skin Tone Enhancement** warms and balances human faces, while **Sharpening** makes textures pop. Use both sparingly to avoid unnatural, gritty footage.
- **Paste Attributes** is a critical feature that allows you to copy your exact adjustment settings from one clip and apply them to others, ensuring visual consistency across your entire video.

modules.title: Speed Control and Keyframe Animation
modules.content:
## The Power of Time and Motion

Imagine watching a skateboarder approach a ramp. They pedal slowly, building tension. Suddenly, they launch into the air, and for a brief, breathtaking moment, time seems to stop. They drop back down, and the video instantly fast-forwards into a blur of motion before settling back to normal speed. 

This dynamic shift in pacing—known as a speed ramp—was once a complex technique reserved for high-end Hollywood editing suites. Today, it is a staple of social media videos, sports highlights, and travel vlogs. But manipulating time is only half the magic. What if you want a photo to slowly zoom in on a subject's face, or a piece of text to drift smoothly across the screen? 

To achieve these effects, we need to master two fundamental concepts: **speed control** (which dictates how fast or slow your clip plays) and **keyframe animation** (which records changes in a clip’s position, size, or angle over time). By combining the timeline skills you learned in *Core Cutting and Sequencing* with these new tools, you will unlock the ability to create highly dynamic, professional-looking videos entirely within CapCut.

## Mastering the Speed Tool

When you film a video, the camera captures a certain number of pictures (or **frames**) every second. Standard video is usually captured at 30 frames per second (fps). When you play it back, the video player shows those 30 frames in sequence over one second, creating the illusion of normal, real-time motion. 

The **speed tool** simply changes how many of those frames are displayed per second. If you speed up a clip to 2x, CapCut squeezes 60 frames of footage into a single second, making the action look twice as fast. If you slow a clip down to 0.5x, CapCut stretches those frames out, taking two seconds to show what originally happened in one.

### Speeding Up and Slowing Down

Adjusting the overall speed of a clip is straightforward and affects the entire duration of the clip.

1. Open your project and tap on the video clip in your **Timeline** to select it. 
2. Tap the **Speed** icon in the bottom toolbar.
3. Select **Normal** from the menu that appears.
4. A slider will appear with numbers ranging from 0.1x to 100x. Drag the slider to the right to speed up the clip, or to the left to slow it down. 
5. Tap the checkmark in the bottom right corner to apply the change.

Notice what happens to your clip on the Timeline. If you speed a clip up, it physically shrinks on the Timeline because it takes up less time. If you slow it down, the clip stretches out. 

*A practical use case:* In *Importing and Organizing Media*, you might have brought in a 30-second clip of a sunset. Speeding it up to 10x turns that slow sunset into a striking 3-second time-lapse. Conversely, if you have a fast-paced sports clip, slowing it down to 0.5x lets the viewer appreciate the mechanics of the movement.

### The Audio Consideration

When you change the speed of a clip, CapCut automatically changes the speed of the audio attached to it. A 2x speed clip will sound like a chipmunk, while a 0.5x clip will sound like a deep, slow drone. 

If you want to keep the video speed changes but don't want the distorted audio, you have two options:
*   **Extract and Detach:** As covered in *Audio Integration and Sound Design*, you can extract the audio, detach it from the video, and delete the distorted audio track, replacing it with background music.
*   **Mute the Clip:** Tap the video clip, select **Volume**, and drag the slider to zero. You can then add a new audio track underneath.

## Creating Speed Ramps with the Speed Curve

Setting a clip to a single speed (like 2x) is useful, but it lacks cinematic flair. True visual impact comes from **speed ramping**—the process of changing the speed *within* the same clip. Think of a car accelerating from a standstill to a blur, or a punch slowing down to a crawl right before impact, then snapping back to real-time.

CapCut makes this process incredibly intuitive using a feature called the **Speed Curve**.

### Understanding the Speed Curve

Instead of applying one speed to the whole clip, the Speed Curve allows you to draw a graph. The horizontal axis represents time (the length of your clip), and the vertical axis represents speed. A flat line means constant speed. A line going up means the clip is accelerating; a line going down means it is decelerating.

CapCut provides two ways to use the Speed Curve:
1.  **Presets:** Ready-made curves designed for specific effects.
2.  **Custom:** A blank graph where you place points to draw your own speed changes.

### Using Preset Speed Curves

CapCut includes several excellent presets that do the heavy lifting for you. 

1. Tap your video clip in the Timeline and select **Speed**.
2. Tap **Curve** at the top of the speed menu.
3. Swipe through the presets. Two of the most popular are:
    *   **Montage:** Creates a rhythmic pulse of fast and slow motion, great for syncing video to the beat of a song.
    *   **Hero:** Creates a dramatic slow-down in the middle of the clip, perfect for action shots or revealing a subject.
4. Tap a preset to select it, then tap the checkmark to apply.

When you apply a preset, you will notice dashed white lines appear on your clip in the Timeline. These lines indicate where the speed is changing. 

### Building a Custom Speed Ramp

Sometimes, you need precise control over exactly where the speed changes happen. For this, you use the Custom curve.

1. Select your clip, go to **Speed** > **Curve**, and tap **Custom**.
2. Tap **Edit** to open the graph. You will see a straight, flat line representing your clip's normal (1x) speed.
3. The white dots on the line are **control points**. Tap anywhere on the line to add a new control point.
4. Drag a control point up to speed the clip up at that exact moment. Drag it down to slow it down.
5. By placing three points—one at the start, one in the middle, and one at the end—you can create a smooth ramp. Drag the middle point down to 0.5x. The line will form a "U" shape. 
6. Tap the checkmark to apply.

**A Note on Smoothness:** When you drag a control point, you might see small handles on either side of the dot. Dragging these handles allows you to round out the corners of your line, making the transition from fast to slow much smoother, rather than an abrupt, jarring stop.

### The Optical Flow Caveat

When you slow a video down significantly, CapCut has to invent new frames to fill the gaps, which can sometimes result in a blurry, jittery look. To combat this, CapCut has a feature called **Optical Flow**. 

Found under the main Speed menu, Optical Flow uses artificial intelligence to analyze the pixels in your video and generate brand-new, realistic frames for the slow-motion sections. It makes slow motion incredibly smooth. However, it is highly processing-intensive. If your video clip is very long or your device is older (as mentioned in the *A note on system requirements* from Chapter 1), Optical Flow might cause CapCut to lag or crash. It is best used on short clips (under 5-10 seconds).

## Understanding Keyframes

If the speed tool controls *when* things happen, keyframes control *where* and *how* things happen. 

A **keyframe** is a marker that locks in a specific property of a clip at a specific point in time. The concept originates from traditional hand-drawn animation. In the early days of animation, master artists would only draw the "key" frames—the starting pose of a character and the ending pose. They would then hand the drawings to junior artists, who would draw all the frames in between to make the motion smooth. 

CapCut acts as your junior artist. You tell CapCut where a clip should start, and where it should end, and the software automatically calculates all the frames in between. This process is called **interpolation**.

### The Keyframe Icon

To use keyframes, you need to find the keyframe icon. It looks like a small diamond shape (♦️). 

1. Tap a video clip in your Timeline.
2. Look at the bottom toolbar. The keyframe icon is usually located between the **Split** and **Filter** icons. 
3. When you tap the icon, a red diamond appears on the clip right above the playhead (the white vertical line that shows your current position in the Timeline). This is your starting keyframe.

## Animating with Keyframes

By placing keyframes at different points in time and changing the clip's properties between them, you can animate three main characteristics: **Scale** (size), **Position** (location), and **Rotation** (angle).

### The "Ken Burns" Effect (Animating Scale and Position)

The most common use of keyframes for beginners is the "Ken Burns" effect—named after the famous documentary filmmaker who popularized the technique of slowly panning and zooming across still photographs. It adds life and motion to static images or video clips.

Let’s animate a slow zoom-in on a subject's face.

1. **Set the Start Point:** Move your playhead to the very beginning of your clip. Tap the **Keyframe** (diamond) icon. You will see a red diamond appear. 
2. **Lock the Starting State:** At this first keyframe, use two fingers to pinch the video in the preview screen to zoom out slightly. Move the video so your subject is framed where you want the animation to start. *Because you just placed a keyframe, CapCut is recording this exact size and position.*
3. **Move the Playhead:** Drag the playhead to the end of the clip (or wherever you want the animation to end).
4. **Set the End State:** Use your fingers to zoom in on the subject's face in the preview screen. Move the video to center the face.
5. **Automatic Keyframing:** Notice that as soon as you change the size or position, CapCut automatically drops a second red diamond onto your clip. 

Press play. CapCut will smoothly interpolate the frames, creating a seamless, slow push-in toward the subject's face. 

### Animating Rotation

You can use the exact same logic to animate the rotation of a clip, which is great for creating dreamy, disorienting transitions or playful sticker movements.

1. Move the playhead to the start of the clip and tap the **Keyframe** icon.
2. Scroll the bottom toolbar and tap **Edit**. Here you will find the **Rotate** tool.
3. Ensure the rotation is at 0 at this first keyframe.
4. Move the playhead to the end of the clip.
5. Change the Rotate value to 90 (or any number you like). A second keyframe will automatically appear.
6. Press play. The clip will smoothly spin 90 degrees over the duration of the video.

### Managing and Deleting Keyframes

As you become more comfortable with keyframes, your clips might accumulate several of them. Managing them is easy:

*   **Navigating:** Tapping a red diamond on your Timeline selects it. You can use the small arrows on either side of the keyframe icon in the toolbar to jump from one keyframe to the next without scrubbing the playhead.
*   **Moving:** You can tap and drag a red diamond left or right along the clip to change when the animation starts or stops. 
*   **Deleting:** If you want to remove a keyframe, tap the red diamond on the Timeline so it is highlighted, then tap the **Trash Can** icon in the toolbar. Alternatively, if the playhead is resting on an existing keyframe, the diamond icon in the toolbar will have a small minus sign next to it; tapping it will delete that specific keyframe.

## Combining Speed and Keyframes

The true magic of video editing happens when you stack techniques. Speed control and keyframe animation are incredibly powerful when used together.

**Scenario: The Action Highlight**
Imagine you have a 5-second clip of a friend throwing a basketball. 
1. First, use a **Custom Speed Curve** to slow the clip down to 0.3x right as the ball leaves their hand. This creates a dramatic slow-motion moment.
2. Because the clip is now playing in slow motion during the middle, the viewer has time to appreciate the details.
3. Next, apply **Keyframes** to the Scale of the clip. Start zoomed out, and slowly zoom in on the basketball as it flies through the air in slow motion. 
4. As the speed curve ramps back up to normal speed at the end of the clip, stop the zoom.

By combining a speed ramp with a keyframe zoom, you have directed the viewer's eye exactly where you want it to go, right at the most exciting moment of the action. This layered approach is how beginners transition into experienced editors—treating effects not as isolated tricks, but as tools that work together to tell a better visual story.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Speed Control:** The Normal speed tool changes the playback speed of an entire clip, physically shrinking (speeding up) or stretching (slowing down) the clip on the Timeline.
*   **Audio Distortion:** Changing video speed automatically alters audio pitch. You can mute the clip or detach the audio to solve this.
*   **Speed Ramping:** The Speed Curve tool allows you to change speeds *within* a single clip, creating dynamic ramps from fast to slow.
*   **Presets vs. Custom:** CapCut offers ready-made Speed Curve presets (like Montage and Hero) or a Custom graph where you can place control points to draw your own speed changes.
*   **Keyframe Definition:** A keyframe is a marker that locks in a specific visual property (like size or position) at a specific point in time. CapCut automatically calculates the frames in between.
*   **Animating Properties:** By placing a keyframe, moving the playhead, and then changing the clip's Scale (zoom), Position (pan), or Rotation, you create smooth, custom animations.
*   **Layering Effects:** For maximum impact, combine speed ramps with keyframe animations to direct the viewer's attention during dynamic moments.

modules.title: Exporting and Sharing the Final Video
modules.content:
You have spent hours perfecting your project. You organized your clips, mastered the core cutting, synced your audio, added dynamic text, and polished the colors. The timeline looks exactly how you envisioned it. But right now, your video only exists inside the CapCut app. To let the world see your creation, you need to translate that timeline into a single, watchable video file. 

This process is called **exporting** (or **rendering**). Think of it as a chef finally plating a meal and sending it out to the dining room. All the ingredients and cooking steps are done; now it is time to deliver the dish. 

However, how you export matters. A video meant for a cinematic YouTube breakdown requires different settings than a quick, punchy clip meant for TikTok. If you choose the wrong settings, your hard work could end up blurry, pixelated, or surrounded by awkward black bars. 

Here is how to configure your export settings and render your final video for any platform.

## The Final Pre-Flight Check

Before you hit the export button, it is highly recommended to do one final review of your timeline. Rendering a video forces your device to process every single effect, transition, and overlay you have added. If you render a video with a mistake, you will have to fix the error and wait for the entire video to render all over again.

Run through this quick checklist:

1. **Watch from start to finish:** Play the video at normal speed. Keep an eye out for stray clips, audio pops, or text that disappears too quickly.
2. **Check for "black bars":** Ensure your clips fill the frame. If you see black spaces around your video, your **Aspect Ratio** is likely mismatched with your footage.
3. **Verify audio levels:** Make sure your background music doesn't drown out your dialogue. 
4. **Look for watermarks:** If you used any "Pro" features by mistake, CapCut will place a watermark on your video unless you upgrade. You will need to delete the specific effect or sticker causing the watermark before exporting.

## Understanding Resolution and Frame Rate

When you are ready to export, you will be presented with a menu of technical settings. To the uninitiated, this looks like a jumble of numbers. But these numbers dictate exactly how your video will look and feel.

### Selecting the Right Resolution

**Resolution** refers to the physical size of your video, measured in pixels. A pixel is a tiny dot of light on your screen; millions of them together form an image. More pixels mean a sharper, clearer image. 

As discussed when setting up your project, CapCut offers three primary resolutions:

*   **720p (1280 x 720):** This is Standard High Definition (HD). It results in a smaller file size, which is useful if you have very limited storage space on your device. However, it will look noticeably soft or slightly blurry on modern televisions and large monitors.
*   **1080p (1920 x 1080):** This is Full High Definition (Full HD). It is the industry standard for web video. It offers an excellent balance of sharp image quality and manageable file size. For 90% of beginner projects, 1080p is the sweet spot.
*   **4K (3840 x 2160):** This is Ultra High Definition (Ultra HD). It offers four times the detail of 1080p. However, 4K files are massive. They take up a lot of storage space, take longer to render, and can cause older devices to stutter during playback. Only choose 4K if your original footage was recorded in 4K and you need the highest possible level of detail.

**A note on "upscaling":** You cannot magically turn low-quality footage into high-quality footage. If you recorded your video in 720p, exporting it in 4K will not make it sharper. CapCut will just stretch the image to fit a larger canvas, often making it look worse. Always match your export resolution to the quality of your original footage, or choose a resolution slightly higher to future-proof the video.

### Selecting the Right Frame Rate

While resolution dictates the *size* of the video, **Frame Rate** dictates the *motion*. 

A video is not actually one continuous recording; it is a series of individual photographs (frames) played back rapidly in sequence. Frame rate, measured in **Frames Per Second (FPS)**, tells you how many of those photographs are displayed every second.

*   **24 FPS:** This is the traditional frame rate for cinema. It has a slightly softer, more artistic motion blur that our brains naturally associate with movies. If you are editing a dramatic short film or a moody travel vlog, 24 FPS is a great choice.
*   **30 FPS:** This is the standard for television broadcasts, news, and standard YouTube videos. It looks smooth and natural for everyday recording. If you aren't sure what to choose, 30 FPS is the safest default.
*   **60 FPS:** This captures twice as many frames per second. The result is incredibly smooth, fluid motion. It is heavily used for video game footage and fast-paced sports. If you used **Speed Control** to slow down fast action, having a 60 FPS source clip allows the slow motion to remain smooth rather than choppy.

To choose your frame rate, simply match the frame rate your camera or phone was set to when you recorded the footage. 

## Choosing the Correct Aspect Ratio

The **Aspect Ratio** is the proportional relationship between the width and the height of your video. In Chapter 1, we briefly introduced the three main ratios. Now, it is time to choose the right one for your specific destination.

### 16:9 (Horizontal)

This is the traditional rectangle shape of televisions and computer monitors. 
*   **Best for:** YouTube, website embeds, television, and presentations.
*   **The Vibe:** Professional, cinematic, and immersive.

### 9:16 (Vertical)

This is the tall, vertical rectangle shape. 
*   **Best for:** TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, and Facebook Stories.
*   **The Vibe:** Mobile-first, intimate, and immediate. 

### 1:1 (Square)

This is a perfect square.
*   **Best for:** Instagram feed posts (though less common now) and Facebook timeline posts.
*   **The Vibe:** Symmetrical and clean. 

### A Realistic Scenario: Repurposing Content

Imagine you edited a beautiful 10-minute travel vlog in a **16:9** aspect ratio for YouTube. You decide you also want to post a 60-second highlight reel to TikTok. 

If you simply export the 16:9 video and upload it to TikTok, the app will shrink your wide video to fit the tall screen, resulting in massive black bars above and below your video. This is called **pillarboxing**. It looks unprofessional and wastes valuable screen real estate.

Instead of forcing a square peg into a round hole, the best practice is to duplicate your project, change the aspect ratio to **9:16**, and use CapCut's canvas tools to reposition your clips. You may need to zoom in on your footage or use the background blur effect to fill the empty vertical space. Always design your edit for the specific screen it will be viewed on.

## Step-by-Step: Exporting to Your Device

Once your pre-flight check is complete and you understand your resolution, frame rate, and aspect ratio, it is time to render the video and save it to your device.

1. **Locate the Export Button:** Look at the top right corner of the CapCut screen. You will see an arrow pointing upwards, usually accompanied by a number indicating the resolution (e.g., "1080p"). Tap this icon.
2. **The Export Settings Menu:** A new menu will drop down. Here you will see sliders or selectable options for **Resolution** and **Frame rate**. 
3. **Adjust Your Settings:** Tap the resolution and frame rate options to select your desired settings (e.g., 1080p and 30 FPS). 
4. **Check the Codecs (Advanced):** On some versions of CapCut, you might see an option for "Codecs" or "Smart HDR". For beginners, it is best to leave these on their default settings. They handle how the video file is compressed.
5. **Initiate the Export:** Tap the prominent "Export" button at the bottom of the menu. 
6. **The Rendering Process:** CapCut will now process your timeline. You will see a progress bar indicating the percentage completed. Do not close the app or switch to another app while this is happening, as it can interrupt the process and corrupt the file.
7. **Save to Camera Roll/Gallery:** Once the progress bar reaches 100%, CapCut will display a success screen. On mobile devices, the video is usually saved directly to your phone's Photos app or Gallery. On desktop, you will be prompted to choose a destination folder on your hard drive.

You will also be presented with options to share the video immediately. 

## Sharing Directly to Social Media

CapCut is uniquely designed for the social media age. Because it is owned by ByteDance—the same parent company as TikTok—it features deep integration with social platforms, allowing you to bypass the step of saving the file to your device entirely if you wish.

### The Direct Share Method

After your video finishes rendering (or directly from the main menu), you will see a row of social media icons. 

*   **TikTok:** Tapping the TikTok icon will open the TikTok app directly, with your freshly exported video loaded into the upload screen. Because CapCut and TikTok share an ecosystem, any CapCut templates or effects you used are often recognized by TikTok, sometimes even boosting the video's visibility in the algorithm.
*   **Instagram:** You can share directly to your Instagram feed, Stories, or Reels. CapCut will automatically format the video based on the aspect ratio you selected.
*   **YouTube:** You can push videos directly to YouTube Shorts or standard YouTube videos. 

**When to use direct share:** Direct sharing is incredibly convenient for quick, mobile-first content. It saves storage space on your phone because the video file doesn't have to sit in your camera roll before you upload it. 

**When to save to device first:** If you are uploading to a platform that isn't listed in CapCut's share menu (like a website or a cloud drive), or if you want to keep a high-quality master copy of the video on your hard drive for future use, you should always save the file to your device first. 

## Common Export Problems and How to Fix Them

Even with perfect settings, rendering can occasionally hit snags. Here are the most common issues beginners face and how to solve them.

### "Storage Full" Error
Video files are large. If your phone or computer runs out of storage space mid-render, the export will fail. 
*   **The Fix:** Check your device's storage before exporting a large 4K video. Delete old unused apps or clear out your photo gallery. If you are on a computer, ensure your hard drive has at least 10% of its total space free for the system to operate efficiently during the render.

### The App Crashes During Render
Rendering is the most intensive task your device will perform while editing. It pushes the processor to its limits. If you have a timeline packed with heavy **Overlays, Stickers, and Effects**, the app might crash.
*   **The Fix:** Try lowering your export resolution. If you are trying to export in 4K, drop it down to 1080p. If it still crashes, try restarting your device to clear the RAM, or temporarily disable some of the heavier visual effects on your timeline.

### The Video Looks Blurry on Social Media
You export a crisp, beautiful 1080p video, but when you upload it to Instagram or TikTok, it looks pixelated and muddy.
*   **The Fix:** This is a result of platform compression. Social media platforms aggressively compress video files to save server space. To combat this, ensure your **Frame rate** is set to at least 30 FPS, and try exporting in a slightly higher bitrate if the option is available. Also, avoid using rapid, flashing transitions, as compression algorithms struggle to process them, resulting in heavy pixelation.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Exporting (Rendering)** is the process of combining all your timeline elements into a single, playable video file.
*   Always perform a **Pre-Flight Check** to catch mistakes before the time-consuming rendering process begins.
*   **Resolution** dictates video size. **1080p** is the standard for web video, while **4K** is for high-detail projects but requires significant storage space. Match your export resolution to your original footage quality.
*   **Frame Rate (FPS)** dictates motion. Use **24 FPS** for a cinematic look, **30 FPS** for standard web video, and **60 FPS** for smooth action and slow motion.
*   **Aspect Ratio** must match your intended platform: **16:9** for YouTube, **9:16** for TikTok/Reels, and **1:1** for standard social feeds. Repurpose content by changing the ratio rather than uploading mismatched dimensions.
*   You can **export directly to your device's camera roll or hard drive**, or use CapCut's direct integration to share immediately to platforms like TikTok and Instagram.
*   If an export fails, check your device's storage space, lower the resolution, or restart your device to free up processing power.

# Chapter 1: Introduction to CapCut and the Editing Interface

Imagine capturing a perfect 30-second clip of your friend skateboarding, a breathtaking sunset, or your toddler’s first steps. The raw footage is great, but it contains 10 seconds of you fumbling with the camera, a shaky pan at the beginning, and a sudden cut-off at the end. To transform that raw footage into a shareable moment, you need a tool to trim the fat, keep the good parts, and polish the final result. 

Enter CapCut.

CapCut is a free, wildly popular video editing application developed by ByteDance (the same company behind TikTok). It has rapidly become the go-to editor for beginners and casual creators because it strips away the overwhelming complexity of professional software while retaining a surprisingly powerful set of tools. Whether you want to make short-form vertical videos for social media or long-form horizontal videos for YouTube, CapCut provides an intuitive workspace to bring your ideas to life.

Before you can start slicing and dicing footage, you need to get the software onto your device and learn the language of the editing interface. This chapter will guide you through downloading CapCut, starting your very first project, and decoding the fundamental terminology of video editing.

## Getting CapCut on Your Device

One of CapCut’s biggest advantages is its cross-platform availability. You can edit on your smartphone while commuting, or sit down at a desktop computer for a more precise, large-screen experience. The core concepts remain the same across all devices, though the layout differs slightly.

### Downloading on Mobile (iOS and Android)

If you are using a smartphone or tablet, CapCut is incredibly easy to acquire. 

1. Open your device’s official app store: the **App Store** for Apple devices (iPhone/iPad) or the **Google Play Store** for Android devices.
2. Tap the search bar and type "CapCut."
3. Look for the app with the signature black icon and a stylized blade/image symbol. The developer should be listed as ByteDance.
4. Tap **Get** (iOS) or **Install** (Android). 
5. Once installed, tap **Open** to launch the app. 

You can use the app immediately without an account, though CapCut will occasionally prompt you to log in via TikTok, Google, or Apple to sync projects across devices.

### Downloading on Desktop (Windows and Mac)

If you prefer a traditional editing setup with a mouse and keyboard, the desktop version of CapCut is available for both Windows and Mac computers.

1. Open your web browser and navigate to the official CapCut website (www.capcut.com).
2. The homepage will typically display a prominent "Download" button. It should automatically detect your operating system (Windows or Mac).
3. Click the button to download the installer file.
4. Once the download is complete, open the installer and follow the on-screen prompts to drag the app to your Applications folder (Mac) or run the setup wizard (Windows).

**A note on system requirements:** While CapCut is relatively lightweight compared to professional editors like Adobe Premiere Pro, it still requires a moderately capable computer to play back video smoothly. If your computer is older than five or six years, you may experience some lag when previewing your edits, though the final exported video will usually turn out fine.

## Starting Your First Project

Once you have CapCut open on your preferred device, the first thing you will see is the **Home Screen**. 

On mobile, this screen displays a large "New Project" button at the top, followed by a grid of your previously saved projects (which will be empty right now). On desktop, you will see a similar layout with a prominent "New project" button.

To begin editing, you need to create a project and bring media into it.

1. Click or tap the **New Project** button.
2. Your device’s file browser or camera roll will open. 
3. Select one or two short video clips or photos from your device. 
4. Tap or click **Add** (mobile) or **Open** (desktop) in the bottom right corner.

You are now looking at the main editing workspace. Before we learn what to do with these clips, we need to understand the language of the workspace itself.

## The Language of Video Editing

Video editing has its own jargon. If you understand a few foundational terms, navigating CapCut—and eventually any other video editing software—becomes much easier. Let’s define the most critical terms you will encounter.

### Resolution

**Resolution** refers to the physical size of your video, measured in pixels. A pixel is a tiny dot of light on your screen; thousands of them together make up an image. Resolution is usually expressed as width x height.

*   **1080p (1920 x 1080):** Also known as Full HD, this is the standard resolution for most modern screens and YouTube videos. 
*   **720p (1280 x 720):** Standard HD. It takes up less storage space but is noticeably less sharp than 1080p.
*   **4K (3840 x 2160):** Ultra HD. This is a massive, highly detailed resolution often used by modern smartphones and professional cameras. Editing 4K requires a powerful device.

When you export your final video, you will choose a resolution. For now, just know that higher resolution means a larger, sharper picture, but also larger file sizes.

### Aspect Ratio

While resolution is about the *size* of the video, **Aspect Ratio** is about the *shape* of the video. It is expressed as a ratio of width to height.

*   **16:9:** The standard horizontal rectangle. Think of a television or a YouTube video.
*   **9:16:** The standard vertical rectangle. Think of TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts.
*   **1:1:** A perfect square. Often used for Instagram feed posts.

CapCut allows you to change your project's aspect ratio at any time, which is incredibly useful if you decide to repurpose a horizontal YouTube video into a vertical TikTok.

### The Timeline

The **Timeline** is the heart of your editing workspace. It is the horizontal area at the bottom of your screen (or across the bottom half on desktop) where you arrange your video clips, audio, and text. 

Think of the timeline as a conveyor belt or a digital tape measure. As you move from left to right, you are moving forward in time through your video. The further right a clip is placed on the timeline, the later it will appear in your final video.

### The Playhead

The **Playhead** is a vertical line that moves across your timeline. It acts as your playback marker, indicating exactly which frame of the video you are currently looking at. 

*   When you press the Play button, the playhead moves from left to right, playing your video in real-time.
*   When you click or tap on a specific spot on the timeline, you manually move the playhead to that exact moment. 

Everything to the left of the playhead has already played, and everything to the right is yet to come.

### Tracks

When you import a video clip, it sits on a horizontal lane called a **Track**. A timeline can have multiple tracks stacked on top of one another. 

Think of tracks like transparent sheets laid on top of each other on an overhead projector. 
*   Your main video clips usually sit on the **Main Track** (sometimes called the primary video track). 
*   You can add **Overlay Tracks** above the main track. If you put a video on an overlay track, it will play on top of the main video at the exact same time. 
*   Below the video tracks, you will usually find **Audio Tracks** where music, sound effects, and voiceovers live.

Understanding tracks is vital because it allows you to layer elements—like playing background music while a video plays, or showing two video clips at once.

## Touring the CapCut Workspace

Now that you know the terminology, let's look at where everything is located. Whether you are on mobile or desktop, the CapCut workspace is divided into three main areas: the Preview Window, the Timeline, and the Tool Panels.

### The Preview Window

Located at the top of your screen (or top half, on desktop), the **Preview Window** is exactly what it sounds like. It is the video player that shows you what is happening at the current position of the playhead. 

If you make a cut, add a filter, or insert text, the results are immediately reflected in the Preview Window. This is your canvas. You will use it to visually confirm that your edits look good before finalizing them. You can also press the Play button beneath or beside this window to watch your sequence back.

### The Timeline

Directly below the Preview Window sits the **Timeline**. As mentioned earlier, this is where you do the actual building of your video. 

When you selected your initial media files to start this project, CapCut automatically placed them side-by-side on the Main Track of the timeline. You will see them represented as rectangular blocks with thumbnail images inside them. The length of the block corresponds to the duration of the clip. A 10-second clip will look twice as long as a 5-second clip.

On the desktop version, you will notice a ruler-like line above your clips with numbers (usually representing seconds). This helps you see exactly how long your clips are. On mobile, you can zoom in and out of the timeline by pinching two fingers together or apart on the screen, allowing you to get a microscopic view for precise edits or a bird's-eye view of a long video.

### The Tool Panels

The **Tool Panels** are where the magic happens. This is where you find the buttons and menus to actually manipulate the clips on your timeline. 

**On Mobile:** The primary Tool Panel is a horizontal menu located directly beneath the selected clip on your timeline. When you tap a clip, this menu pops up, offering options like **Split**, **Speed**, **Volume**, and **Delete**. Above the timeline, near the Preview Window, you will find secondary menus for adding new elements: **Audio**, **Text**, **Stickers**, and **Effects**.

**On Desktop:** The layout is more traditional. 
*   At the very top of the screen, you have a menu bar with tabs like **Media**, **Audio**, **Text**, **Transitions**, and **Effects**. Clicking these opens specific panels in the top-left corner of your screen, allowing you to add new elements to your timeline.
*   In the top-right corner, directly above the Preview Window, is the **Inspector Panel** (sometimes called the Properties Panel). When you click on a clip in your timeline, this panel changes to show you the specific adjustments you can make to *that exact clip*—such as volume, speed, color correction, or animation settings.

## A Realistic Scenario: Trimming the Fat

To see how these elements work together, imagine you imported a 15-second clip of your friend skateboarding. You watch it in the Preview Window and realize the first 4 seconds are just you walking to get into position, and the last 3 seconds are you lowering the camera. The actual trick only happens in the middle 8 seconds.

Here is how you would use the workspace to fix it, applying the terms we just learned:

1.  **Locate the start:** Drag your **Playhead** across the **Timeline** until the **Preview Window** shows the exact moment you stop walking and the skateboarder is about to push off.
2.  **Make the first cut:** Select the clip, then tap the **Split** tool (represented by a scissors icon on mobile, or a split button on desktop). This slices the clip into two separate pieces at the playhead.
3.  **Delete the bad footage:** Select the first piece (the 4 seconds of walking) and tap **Delete** (or **Remove**). The clip vanishes from the **Main Track**, and the rest of the video snaps to the left to fill the gap.
4.  **Locate the end:** Drag the **Playhead** to the moment the skateboarder lands the trick, right before you lower the camera. 
5.  **Make the second cut:** Use the **Split** tool again to slice the clip.
6.  **Delete the final bad footage:** Select the last piece (the 3 seconds of camera lowering) and delete it.

In less than a minute, you have used the Timeline, Playhead, Preview Window, and Tool Panel to turn a messy 15-second clip into a tight, 8-second action shot. While Chapter 3 will dive deep into the mechanics of cutting, this scenario illustrates how the interface is designed to facilitate quick, visual storytelling.

## Key Takeaways

*   **CapCut is cross-platform:** It is available for free on iOS, Android, Windows, and Mac, making professional-grade editing tools accessible to anyone.
*   **Resolution defines size, Aspect Ratio defines shape:** Resolution (like 1080p) is the pixel count of your video, while Aspect Ratio (like 16:9 or 9:16) dictates the shape of the frame for different platforms.
*   **The Timeline is your digital workbench:** It operates from left to right, representing the passage of time in your video. 
*   **The Playhead is your guide:** This vertical line shows you exactly where you are in the video, allowing you to navigate to specific frames for precise editing.
*   **Tracks allow for layering:** The Main Track holds your primary video, while Overlay and Audio tracks let you stack additional media and sound on top of one another.
*   **The workspace is divided into three areas:** The Preview Window (your visual canvas), the Timeline (where you arrange media), and the Tool Panels (where you access features to alter the media).

# Chapter 2: Importing and Organizing Media

## Gathering Your Building Blocks

Imagine you are a chef preparing to cook a complex meal. Before you ever turn on the stove, you must gather, wash, and chop your ingredients, laying them out in bowls on the counter so they are within arm's reach when the cooking gets fast. Video editing works exactly the same way. Before you make a single cut, you need to gather your raw video clips, images, and audio files, and arrange them neatly in your editing workspace. 

In the previous chapter, we explored the CapCut interface, learning how to create a New Project and navigate the Timeline, Playhead, and Preview Window. Now, it is time to bring your actual media into that workspace. In video editing, the term **media** refers to any digital file you use to build your video—this includes video clips, photographs, graphics, and audio files like music or voice recordings. 

Understanding how to import media efficiently and organize it before you start cutting will save you hours of frustration. When your "ingredients" are scattered, editing feels chaotic. When they are organized, the creative process becomes smooth and enjoyable.

## Sourcing Your Media: Where Do Videos Come From?

Before you can organize your media, you have to bring it into CapCut. The app offers several ways to do this, depending on where your files currently live. When you open a New Project, you are immediately greeted with a prompt to add media. You generally have three primary sources: your device’s local storage, cloud storage, and stock libraries.

### Importing from Your Device Gallery

The most common way to start is by pulling files directly from the device you are using—whether that is a smartphone, tablet, or computer. 

When you tap the **Add** or **Import** button, CapCut opens your device’s native gallery or file browser. From here, you can simply tap on the files you want to use. 
*   **Multi-selecting:** You do not have to import clips one by one. You can tap multiple clips or images at once. A checkmark usually appears on the items you have selected.
*   **Order of selection matters:** When you select multiple clips from your device gallery and bring them into CapCut, they will appear in the app in the exact order you tapped them. If you want Clip A to come before Clip B, make sure you tap Clip A first.

Once you have selected your files, tap the **Add** or **Import** button (usually in the bottom right corner of the screen) to drop them into your project.

### Importing from Cloud Storage

If you are editing on a mobile device, your phone’s storage might be limited, or your files might live on a different device entirely. This is where cloud storage comes in. **Cloud storage** is a service that holds your files on remote servers rather than on your physical device, allowing you to access them via the internet.

CapCut allows you to connect to cloud services (like Google Drive, iCloud, or Dropbox, depending on your device) to import media. 
1. In the import menu, look for the cloud icon or the option to browse files from other locations.
2. Navigate to your connected cloud service.
3. Select the video or image you want to download into your project.

**A note on cloud media:** When you import a file from the cloud, CapCut has to download a copy of it to your device to edit it. If you have a slow internet connection, this may take a moment. Ensure the file is fully downloaded before you start editing, otherwise the app might lag.

### Using CapCut’s Stock Libraries

Sometimes you do not have the perfect b-roll (supplementary footage) to support your main video, or you need background music but do not own the rights to any songs. CapCut provides built-in **stock libraries**, which are collections of royalty-free media provided directly by the app for you to use.

You can access these libraries from the main import screen or from the Tool Panels below the Timeline:
*   **Stock Video:** High-quality, generic video clips (like a drone shot of a city, or a close-up of coffee pouring) that you can drop into your project to cover cuts or illustrate a point.
*   **Stock Images:** Photographs and graphics you can use as backgrounds or visual aids.
*   **Stock Audio:** A library of music tracks and sound effects. 

To use stock media, simply browse the categories, tap on a clip to preview it, and select the download or plus icon to bring it into your project. 

## Understanding Track Hierarchy: Where Does Media Go?

Once you import media, it has to live somewhere in your editing workspace. In Chapter 1, we briefly introduced the concept of Tracks. Now, we need to look at how different types of media interact on these tracks, as this is the foundation of how CapCut organizes your files.

### The Main Video Track

The **Main Track** is the backbone of your video. Think of it as the primary timeline of events. When you import a batch of video clips and drop them onto the timeline, they automatically snap together end-to-end on this Main Track. 

If you have a video of a person talking, that interview footage belongs on the Main Track. It is the base layer. If you delete a clip from the Main Track, the subsequent clips will slide over to fill the gap, keeping the timeline continuous. Every project must have a Main Track; you cannot have a video without it.

### Overlay Tracks

If the Main Track is the foundation of a house, **Overlay Tracks** are the decorations inside the rooms. An overlay is any video or image that plays *on top of* the Main Track. 

Why would you want media on top of other media? 
*   **Picture-in-picture:** Showing a reaction video in a small box in the corner while your main video plays.
*   **B-roll coverage:** Showing a video of a beautiful sunset while a voiceover from the Main Track continues to play underneath.
*   **Graphics:** Placing a logo or a watermark in the corner of your screen.

When you add an overlay, it sits on a track directly above the Main Track. Overlays do not push Main Track clips out of the way. Instead, they float above them. You can have multiple Overlay Tracks stacked on top of each other, allowing for complex compositions where several videos or images are visible at the same time.

### Audio Tracks

**Audio Tracks** handle the sound elements of your video. Any video clip you place on the Main Track automatically brings its original sound with it on the same track. However, when you import standalone audio files—like a downloaded MP3 song or a voice memo you recorded—they are placed on dedicated Audio Tracks, which sit below the Main Video Track. 

Keeping your audio on separate tracks allows you to adjust the volume of your background music independently from your main video’s dialogue.

## Organizing in the Media Panel

When you import media into CapCut, it does not immediately disappear into the timeline forever. It is first stored in the **Media Panel** (sometimes called the Project Panel or Import Panel), which is the library of all the assets you have brought into this specific project. 

On a computer, this panel is usually located in the top left corner of the screen. On a mobile device, it appears as a horizontal scrolling list of thumbnails just above the Timeline.

### Arranging Clips Before Editing

One of the biggest mistakes beginners make is immediately dragging every single clip onto the Timeline and then trying to make sense of the mess. A much more efficient workflow is to arrange and review your clips in the Media Panel *before* bringing them to the Timeline.

Here is a practical scenario: Imagine you just returned from a weekend trip and shot 45 different video clips on your phone. You want to make a one-minute travel montage.

1.  **Import everything:** Bring all 45 clips into the Media Panel, but do not drag them to the Timeline yet.
2.  **Review and trim the fat:** Tap through the clips in the Media Panel. If a clip is entirely blurry or unusable, delete it from the panel right now. 
3.  **Rename for clarity (Desktop):** If you are on a computer, you can often right-click a clip in the Media Panel and rename it. Naming a clip "Sunset_Beach" makes it much easier to find than "IMG_4829.MP4".
4.  **Group by type:** Mentally (or physically, by rearranging the thumbnails) group your clips. Put all your wide landscape shots together, and your close-up food shots together. 

By organizing your media panel first, you create a logical roadmap for your video. When you finally start dragging clips to the Timeline in the next chapter, you will know exactly where everything is.

## The Art of Replacement: Swapping Clips Safely

As you edit, you will often change your mind. You might place a video clip on the Timeline, spend time adjusting its speed, applying a color filter, and perfectly positioning it—only to realize you actually prefer a different, but similar, clip you shot of the same subject.

If you delete the clip and start over, you lose all the edits you just made. Fortunately, CapCut has a powerful feature that solves this: **Replace**.

The Replace function allows you to swap out a clip on the Timeline with a different clip from your Media Panel, while keeping all the edits, effects, and adjustments you applied to the original clip intact.

### How to Use the Replace Function

The exact steps vary slightly depending on whether you are using CapCut on a mobile device or a desktop, but the core concept remains the same.

**On Mobile:**
1. Tap the clip on the Timeline that you want to swap out.
2. In the Tool Panel (the menu of icons below the Timeline), scroll until you find the **Replace** icon (it usually looks like two overlapping squares or a recycling symbol).
3. Tap **Replace**. CapCut will ask if you want to replace the clip from your device gallery or from the stock library.
4. Select your source, then tap the new clip you want to use.
5. CapCut will give you two options: **Replace** or **Overwrite**. 
    *   If you choose standard Replace, the new clip will take on the exact duration and position of the old clip. If the new clip is longer, it will be trimmed to match. If it is shorter, CapCut will slow it down or freeze a frame to match the length (depending on settings).
    *   You can usually drag the new clip left or right within that slot to choose a different starting point before confirming.

**On Desktop:**
1. Right-click the clip on the Timeline you wish to change.
2. Select **Replace** from the drop-down menu.
3. Choose **Replace clip** to swap it with a clip from your Media Panel, or **Replace with placeholder** if you want to leave a blank slot to fill later.
4. Click the new clip from your Media Panel to execute the swap.

### Why Replacement is a Lifesaver

Let’s look at another scenario. You are editing a cooking video. On the Main Track, you have a clip of chopped onions falling into a sizzling pan. You have perfectly timed this clip to be exactly 3 seconds long, and you have added a warm color filter to make the food look appetizing. 

Later, you realize the lighting in that shot was actually a bit too dark, but you have a second take of the exact same action that is brighter. Instead of deleting the dark clip, losing your 3-second timing and your color filter, and starting over with the bright clip, you simply use the **Replace** tool. You select the bright clip from your gallery, hit Replace, and CapCut swaps the video while keeping the 3-second duration and the warm color filter perfectly intact. 

This tool is essentially an "undo" button for your initial importing choices, allowing you to experiment freely without fear of having to redo tedious editing work.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Media** is the umbrella term for all video, image, and audio files used in a project.
*   You can import media from three main sources: your device’s local gallery, cloud storage (which requires an internet connection to download), and CapCut’s built-in stock libraries.
*   The **Main Track** is the foundational layer of your video where primary clips sit end-to-end.
*   **Overlay Tracks** sit above the Main Track and allow videos or images to play simultaneously on screen, useful for picture-in-picture and b-roll.
*   **Audio Tracks** sit below the video tracks and hold your music and sound effects independently of the video.
*   The **Media Panel** acts as your digital ingredient bowl. Organizing, reviewing, and deleting unusable clips in the Media Panel *before* moving them to the Timeline makes the editing process much smoother.
*   The **Replace** tool allows you to swap a clip on the Timeline with a new one from your media library without losing the timing, effects, and adjustments you already applied to the original clip.
